1. Library Dependencies¶

In [1]:
# Environment Setup and Library Imports
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mtick
import seaborn as sns
from scipy.optimize import minimize
from patsy import dmatrix
import copy
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=RuntimeWarning)
from sklearn.exceptions import ConvergenceWarning
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=ConvergenceWarning)
from IPython.display import display

from sklearn.preprocessing import RobustScaler, PolynomialFeatures, LabelEncoder
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.linear_model import (
    LinearRegression, Ridge, Lasso, ElasticNet, HuberRegressor,
    BayesianRidge, QuantileRegressor
)
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsRegressor
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.model_selection import (
    train_test_split, KFold,
    cross_val_score, GridSearchCV, RandomizedSearchCV, learning_curve
)
from sklearn.metrics import r2_score, make_scorer
from sklearn.feature_selection import RFECV
import xgboost as xgb
from sklearn.base import BaseEstimator, RegressorMixin
from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import variance_inflation_factor

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', 60)
pd.set_option('display.float_format', '{:.4f}'.format)
sns.set_theme(style='whitegrid', palette='muted', font_scale=1.0)
plt.rcParams.update({'figure.dpi': 100})

print("All libraries loaded successfully.")

All libraries loaded successfully.

2. Data Import & Cleaning¶

In [2]:
# Load Dataset
data = pd.read_csv('house_dataset.csv')
In [3]:
# Display the Data
print(data.shape)
data.head()

(9200, 18)
Out[3]:
date price bedrooms bathrooms sqft_living sqft_lot floors waterfront view condition sqft_above sqft_basement yr_built yr_renovated street city statezip country
0 2014-05-02 00:00:00 313000.0000 3.0000 1.5000 1340 7912 1.5000 0 0 3 1340 0 1955 2005 18810 Densmore Ave N Shoreline WA 98133 USA
1 2014-05-02 00:00:00 2384000.0000 5.0000 2.5000 3650 9050 2.0000 0 4 5 3370 280 1921 0 709 W Blaine St Seattle WA 98119 USA
2 2014-05-02 00:00:00 342000.0000 3.0000 2.0000 1930 11947 1.0000 0 0 4 1930 0 1966 0 26206-26214 143rd Ave SE Kent WA 98042 USA
3 2014-05-02 00:00:00 420000.0000 3.0000 2.2500 2000 8030 1.0000 0 0 4 1000 1000 1963 0 857 170th Pl NE Bellevue WA 98008 USA
4 2014-05-02 00:00:00 550000.0000 4.0000 2.5000 1940 10500 1.0000 0 0 4 1140 800 1976 1992 9105 170th Ave NE Redmond WA 98052 USA

The raw dataset contains house sale records with features such as bedrooms, bathrooms, square footage, location, condition, and sale price. We'll inspect and clean this before modelling.

In [4]:
# Remove Exact Duplicates Rows
n_before = len(data)
data.drop_duplicates(inplace=True)
data.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)
n_after = len(data)
print(f"Rows before deduplication : {n_before}")
print(f"Rows after deduplication  : {n_after}")
print(f"Duplicates removed        : {n_before - n_after}")
# 4,598 redundant rows were identified and removed  to prevent bias in the predictive model

Rows before deduplication : 9200
Rows after deduplication  : 4602
Duplicates removed        : 4598

Exact duplicate rows were removed to prevent the model from seeing the same transaction twice, which would bias training.

In [5]:
# Remove Invalid Price Records
n_zero_price = (data['price'] == 0).sum()
print(f"Price = 0 records: {n_zero_price}")
data = data[data['price'] > 0].reset_index(drop=True)
print(f"Rows after removing zero prices: {len(data)}")

Price = 0 records: 49
Rows after removing zero prices: 4553

Records with price = 0 are invalid entries (likely data entry errors) and are dropped.

In [6]:
# Remove Zero Bedrooms / Bathrooms
n_zero_rooms = ((data['bedrooms'] == 0) | (data['bathrooms'] == 0)).sum()
print(f"Zero bedroom/bathroom records: {n_zero_rooms}")
data = data[(data['bedrooms'] > 0) & (data['bathrooms'] > 0)].reset_index(drop=True)
print(f"Rows after removing zero rooms: {len(data)}")

Zero bedroom/bathroom records: 2
Rows after removing zero rooms: 4551

Houses with zero bedrooms or bathrooms are physically implausible and removed as data quality issues.

In [7]:
# Fix SQFT Inconsistency
n_inconsistent = (data['sqft_living'] != (data['sqft_above'] + data['sqft_basement'])).sum()
print(f"Inconsistent sqft records: {n_inconsistent}")
data = data[data['sqft_living'] == (data['sqft_above'] + data['sqft_basement'])].reset_index(drop=True)
print(f"Rows after sqft fix: {len(data)}")

Inconsistent sqft records: 2
Rows after sqft fix: 4549

sqft_living must equal sqft_above + sqft_basement by definition. Rows that violate this constraint are dropped as inconsistent records.

In [8]:
# Fix Renovation Year
# yr_renovated = 0 or < yr_built -> replace with yr_built (house was never renovated)
n_bad_reno = ((data['yr_renovated'] == 0) | (data['yr_renovated'] < data['yr_built'])).sum()
print(f"Bad renovation year records: {n_bad_reno}")
data['yr_renovated'] = np.where(
    (data['yr_renovated'] == 0) | (data['yr_renovated'] < data['yr_built']),
    data['yr_built'],
    data['yr_renovated']
)

Bad renovation year records: 2899

When yr_renovated is 0 or earlier than yr_built, we treat the house as never renovated and set yr_renovated = yr_built. This avoids negative or nonsensical renovation ages.

In [9]:
# Deduplicate Repeated Sales (Same Property)
# Properties sold more than once -> keep the most recent transaction only
data['date'] = pd.to_datetime(data['date'])
rows_before = len(data)
data = (data
        .sort_values(['street', 'sqft_living', 'date'], ascending=[True, True, False])
        .drop_duplicates(subset=['street', 'sqft_living'], keep='first')
        .reset_index(drop=True))
print(f"Repeated sales removed: {rows_before - len(data)}")
print(f"Final clean dataset shape: {data.shape}")

Repeated sales removed: 12
Final clean dataset shape: (4537, 18)

For properties sold multiple times, we keep only the most recent transaction to avoid temporal leakage, older sale prices do not reflect current market value.

In [10]:
# Extract Derived Time Features
data['year_sold']             = data['date'].dt.year
data['house_age']             = data['year_sold'] - data['yr_built']
data['years_since_renovation']= data['year_sold'] - data['yr_renovated']
data['zipcode']               = data['statezip'].str.split(' ').str[1]

# Drop redundant raw columns
data.drop(columns=['country', 'statezip', 'street', 'yr_built',
                    'yr_renovated', 'year_sold', 'date'], inplace=True, errors='ignore')

print("Remaining columns:")
print(data.columns.tolist())
print(f"\nFinal shape: {data.shape}")

Remaining columns:
['price', 'bedrooms', 'bathrooms', 'sqft_living', 'sqft_lot', 'floors', 'waterfront', 'view', 'condition', 'sqft_above', 'sqft_basement', 'city', 'house_age', 'years_since_renovation', 'zipcode']

Final shape: (4537, 15)

We derive house_age and years_since_renovation from sale year and build/renovation year. Raw date columns and redundant identifiers are dropped to reduce noise.

In [11]:
# Final Integrity Check
print("Missing values:\n", data.isnull().sum()[data.isnull().sum() > 0])
print(f"\nTotal missing: {data.isnull().sum().sum()}")
print(f"\nUnique waterfront values: {sorted(data['waterfront'].unique())}")
print(f"Unique view values      : {sorted(data['view'].unique())}")
print(f"Unique condition values : {sorted(data['condition'].unique())}")

print("\nData Cleaning Summary")
print(f"  Final clean dataset: {len(data):,} rows × {len(data.columns)} columns")

Missing values:
 Series([], dtype: int64)

Total missing: 0

Unique waterfront values: [np.int64(0), np.int64(1)]
Unique view values      : [np.int64(0), np.int64(1), np.int64(2), np.int64(3), np.int64(4)]
Unique condition values : [np.int64(1), np.int64(2), np.int64(3), np.int64(4), np.int64(5)]

Data Cleaning Summary
  Final clean dataset: 4,537 rows × 15 columns

No missing values remain after cleaning. Ordinal features (waterfront, view, condition) have valid categorical ranges. The dataset is ready for modelling purposes in the next section

3. Target Variable Transformation¶

Log-transform price (log1p) for three reasons:

  1. Normalises the right-skewed distribution (OLS normality assumption)
  2. Stabilises variance (heteroscedasticity) across price ranges
  3. MAPE is evaluated in the original price space after converting the log-transformed predictions back using expm1.
In [12]:
# Log-transform price
data['log_price'] = np.log1p(data['price'])

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 4))
axes[0].hist(data['price'], bins=60, color='#F4A5A0', edgecolor='white')
axes[0].set_title('Original Price (Highly Skewed)', fontsize=12)
axes[0].xaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f'${x/1e6:.1f}M'))
axes[1].hist(data['log_price'], bins=60, color='#A0C4F4', edgecolor='white')
axes[1].set_title('log1p(Price) — Approximately Normal', fontsize=12)
axes[1].set_xlabel('log1p(Price)')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

print(f"Skewness — original: {data['price'].skew():.3f}  |  log-transformed: {data['log_price'].skew():.3f}")

Skewness — original: 25.015  |  log-transformed: 0.328

The log transformation successfully reduces right skew from 25 to near 0, and variance becomes more homogeneous across price ranges. This satisfies OLS assumptions of normality and homoscedasticity.

4. Exploratory Data Analysis¶

In [13]:
# Univariate: Core structural features
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 4))
sns.countplot(data=data, x='bedrooms',  ax=axes[0], palette='viridis')
axes[0].set_title('Bedrooms Distribution')
sns.histplot(data=data, x='bathrooms', bins=12, ax=axes[1], color='#91CAF6')
axes[1].set_title('Bathrooms Distribution')
sns.countplot(data=data, x='floors', ax=axes[2], palette='pastel')
axes[2].set_title('Floors Distribution')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

Most houses have 3–4 bedrooms, 1–3 bathrooms, and 1–2 floors (a typical residential distribution).

In [14]:
# Bivariate: sqft_living vs price (original vs log)
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 4))
sns.regplot(data=data, x='sqft_living', y='price', ax=axes[0],
            scatter_kws={'alpha': 0.2, 's': 10}, line_kws={'color': 'red'})
axes[0].set_title('sqft_living vs Price (original — heteroscedastic)')
sns.regplot(data=data, x='sqft_living', y='log_price', ax=axes[1],
            scatter_kws={'alpha': 0.2, 's': 10, 'color': '#4CAF50'}, line_kws={'color': 'orange'})
axes[1].set_title('sqft_living vs log_price (more linear)')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

The log transformation of price successfully stabilized the variance and clarified the linear relationship with sqft_living. This validated the choice of transformation for the regression.

In [15]:
# Geographic price variation
top10 = data.groupby('city')['price'].mean().sort_values(ascending=False).head(10)
plt.figure(figsize=(11, 4))
sns.barplot(x=top10.values, y=top10.index, palette='Reds_r')
plt.title('Top 10 Most Expensive Cities (Average Price)', fontsize=12)
plt.xlabel('Average Price ($)')
plt.gca().xaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f'${x/1e6:.1f}M'))
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

Cities like Medina, Clyde Hill show dramatically higher average prices, geographic location is clearly a dominant signal. This motivates our spatial feature engineering in Section 6.

In [16]:
# Quality ordinals vs log_price
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 4))
sns.boxplot(data=data, x='condition', y='log_price', ax=axes[0], palette='Pastel1')
axes[0].set_title('log_price by Condition')
sns.boxplot(data=data, x='view', y='log_price', ax=axes[1], palette='Pastel2')
axes[1].set_title('log_price by View Score')
sns.boxplot(data=data, x='waterfront', y='log_price', ax=axes[2], palette='Set2')
axes[2].set_title('log_price by Waterfront')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

View and waterfront features proved to be strong predictors of price, while condition had a weaker but still relevant influence.

In [17]:
# House age distribution & relationship with price
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 4))
sns.histplot(data=data, x='house_age', bins=40, kde=True, ax=axes[0], color='#95C8A0')
axes[0].set_title('House Age Distribution')
sns.scatterplot(data=data, x='house_age', y='log_price', ax=axes[1],
                alpha=0.2, s=10, color='#7B68EE')
axes[1].set_title('House Age vs log_price')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

House age follows a bimodal distribution (many new homes and many older homes). The scatter against log_price shows a weak non-linear relationship — very new and very old homes can both fetch high prices, motivating the is_new and is_historic binary flags we create later.

In [18]:
# Full correlation heatmap
numeric_cols = data.select_dtypes(include=[np.number]).columns.tolist()
corr = data[numeric_cols].corr()
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 11))
sns.heatmap(corr, annot=True, cmap='RdBu_r', center=0, fmt='.2f',
            linewidths=0.3, annot_kws={'size': 8})
plt.title('Full Feature Correlation Matrix', fontsize=13)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

# Top correlations with log_price

print(corr['log_price'].drop('log_price').abs().sort_values(ascending=False).head(12).to_string())

Top features correlated with log_price:
price           0.6772
sqft_living     0.6711
sqft_above      0.5815
bathrooms       0.5525
bedrooms        0.3595
view            0.3236
sqft_basement   0.3132
floors          0.3061
waterfront      0.1420
sqft_lot        0.0856
house_age       0.0597
condition       0.0594

Based on the correlation matrix with log_price, we observe:

  • Size matters most:
    sqft_living (0.6711) and sqft_above (0.5815) show the strongest positive correlations with price.
  • Amenities contribute moderately:
    bathrooms (0.5525) and bedrooms (0.3595) are meaningful predictors of housing price.

5. Train/Test Split¶

In [19]:
city_raw    = data['city'].astype(str).copy()
zipcode_raw = data['zipcode'].astype(str).copy()
log_price_all = data['log_price'].copy()

# Label-encode for baseline model
le = LabelEncoder()
data_ml = data.copy()
data_ml['city']    = le.fit_transform(data_ml['city'].astype(str))
data_ml['zipcode'] = le.fit_transform(data_ml['zipcode'].astype(str))

X_base = data_ml.drop(columns=['price', 'log_price'])
y      = data_ml['log_price']

# Split into train/test sets (80/20) with fixed random state for reproducibility
X_tr_base, X_te_base, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X_base, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=42)

train_idx = X_tr_base.index.tolist()
test_idx  = X_te_base.index.tolist()

print(f"Train: {len(train_idx):,} rows  |  Test: {len(test_idx):,} rows")

Train: 3,629 rows  |  Test: 908 rows

80/20 random split with random_state=42 for reproducibility. City and zipcode are label-encoded here for the baseline OLS only; target encoding is applied separately in the full feature engineering pipeline.

6. Feature Engineering¶

Category Features
A. Baseline Structural 12
B. Non-Linear Basis 8
C. Domain-Informed Metrics 7
D. Categorical & Binary 7
E. Interaction Effects 7
F. Spatial / Geographic 4
In [20]:
# KFold Target Encoding
def kfold_target_encode(series_all, target_all, train_idx, test_idx,
                        n_splits=5, random_state=42):

    encoded = np.zeros(len(series_all))
    s_all = series_all.reset_index(drop=True)
    t_all = target_all.reset_index(drop=True)

    s_train = s_all.iloc[train_idx].reset_index(drop=True)
    t_train = t_all.iloc[train_idx].reset_index(drop=True)
    global_mean = t_train.mean()

    kf = KFold(n_splits=n_splits, shuffle=True, random_state=random_state)
    fold_enc = np.full(len(s_train), np.nan)

    for tr_rel, va_rel in kf.split(s_train):
        means = t_train.iloc[tr_rel].groupby(s_train.iloc[tr_rel]).mean()
        fold_enc[va_rel] = s_train.iloc[va_rel].map(means).fillna(global_mean).values

    for i, idx in enumerate(train_idx):
        encoded[idx] = fold_enc[i]

    full_means = t_train.groupby(s_train).mean()
    for idx in test_idx:
        encoded[idx] = full_means.get(s_all.iloc[idx], global_mean)

    return encoded

print("kfold_target_encode() defined.")

kfold_target_encode() defined.

KFold target encoding computes the mean target per category using out-of-fold data only, preventing target leakage. Test rows are encoded using means computed on the full training set.

In [21]:
# Feature Engineering Pipeline
df = data.copy()
log_price_all = df['log_price'].copy()

# A. Baseline Structural (12)
# bedrooms, bathrooms, sqft_living, sqft_lot, floors,
# waterfront, view, condition, sqft_above, sqft_basement,
# house_age, years_since_renovation  — already in df

# B. Non-Linear Basis Expansion (8)
df['sqft_living_sq']   = df['sqft_living'] ** 2           
df['sqft_living_sqrt'] = np.sqrt(df['sqft_living'])       
df['sqft_living_log']  = np.log1p(df['sqft_living'])      
df['sqft_lot_log']     = np.log1p(df['sqft_lot'])         
df['sqft_above_log']   = np.log1p(df['sqft_above'])       
df['sqft_bsmt_log']    = np.log1p(df['sqft_basement'])    
df['bath_per_bed']     = df['bathrooms'] / (df['bedrooms'] + 0.1)
df['bed_bath_sum']     = df['bedrooms'] + df['bathrooms']

# C. Domain-Informed Structural Metrics (7)
df['lot_utilization']  = df['sqft_living']  / (df['sqft_lot'] + 1)
df['avg_room_size']    = df['sqft_living']  / (df['bed_bath_sum'] + 0.1)
df['has_basement']     = (df['sqft_basement'] > 0).astype(int)
df['above_grade_frac'] = df['sqft_above']   / (df['sqft_living'] + 1)
df['basement_frac']    = df['sqft_basement'] / (df['sqft_living'] + 1)
df['effective_living'] = df['sqft_above'] + 0.5 * df['sqft_basement']  
df['sqft_per_room'] = df['sqft_living'] / (df['bedrooms'] * df['bathrooms'] + 1)

# D. Categorical & Binary Indicators (7)
df['is_luxury']        = ((df['view'] >= 3) & (df['condition'] >= 4)).astype(int)
df['is_renovated']     = (df['years_since_renovation'] < df['house_age']).astype(int)
df['is_historic']      = (df['house_age'] > 75).astype(int)
df['is_new']           = (df['house_age'] <= 5).astype(int)
df['age_category']     = pd.cut(df['house_age'],
                                bins=[-1, 5, 20, 50, 75, 999],
                                labels=[0, 1, 2, 3, 4]).astype(int)
df['waterfront_view']  = df['waterfront'] * df['view']    
df['condition_view']   = df['condition'] * df['view']

# E. Interaction Effects (7)
df['living_x_cond']    = df['sqft_living'] * df['condition']
df['living_x_view']    = df['sqft_living'] * df['view']
df['living_x_wf']      = df['sqft_living'] * df['waterfront']
df['log_living_x_cond']= df['sqft_living_log'] * df['condition']
df['log_living_x_view']= df['sqft_living_log'] * df['view']
df['age_x_cond']       = df['house_age'] * df['condition']  
df['bed_x_sqft']       = df['bedrooms'] * df['sqft_living_log']

print(f"Current df shape: {df.shape}")

Current df shape: (4537, 45)

Categories A–E add polynomial transformations, domain ratios (e.g. bath_per_bed, lot_utilization), interaction terms, and binary flags.

In [22]:
# F. Spatial & Geographic Features (4)

city_series    = city_raw.copy()
zipcode_series = zipcode_raw.copy()

# F1. City-level price signal (KFold target encoding)
df['city_price_signal'] = kfold_target_encode(
    city_series, log_price_all, train_idx, test_idx)

# F2. Zipcode-level price signal
df['zipcode_price_signal'] = kfold_target_encode(
    zipcode_series, log_price_all, train_idx, test_idx)

# F3. KNN Neighbor Price Proxy
# Train rows: each row's proxy is predicted from other training folds
# Test rows : proxy predicted by KNN fitted on full training set
knn_loc_cols  = ['city_price_signal', 'zipcode_price_signal']
knn_proxy     = np.zeros(len(df))

train_arr     = df.iloc[train_idx][knn_loc_cols].values
y_train_arr   = log_price_all.iloc[train_idx].values

kf_knn = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
for tr_rel, va_rel in kf_knn.split(train_arr):
    sc  = RobustScaler()
    knn = KNeighborsRegressor(n_neighbors=10, metric='euclidean', weights='distance')
    knn.fit(sc.fit_transform(train_arr[tr_rel]), y_train_arr[tr_rel])
    knn_proxy[np.array(train_idx)[va_rel]] = knn.predict(sc.transform(train_arr[va_rel]))

sc_f   = RobustScaler()
knn_f  = KNeighborsRegressor(n_neighbors=10, metric='euclidean', weights='distance')
knn_f.fit(sc_f.fit_transform(train_arr), y_train_arr)
knn_proxy[test_idx] = knn_f.predict(sc_f.transform(df.iloc[test_idx][knn_loc_cols].values))

df['neighbor_price_proxy'] = knn_proxy

# F4. KMeans Cluster Label
km_feat_cols = ['city_price_signal', 'zipcode_price_signal', 'sqft_living', 'sqft_lot']
km_feat = df[km_feat_cols].copy()

sc_km = RobustScaler()
km_train_scaled = sc_km.fit_transform(km_feat.iloc[train_idx])
kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=12, random_state=42, n_init=10)
kmeans.fit(km_train_scaled)

df['cluster_label'] = kmeans.predict(sc_km.transform(km_feat))

# verification assessment
corr_proxy = np.corrcoef(
    df.iloc[train_idx]['neighbor_price_proxy'].values,
    log_price_all.iloc[train_idx].values
)[0, 1]
print(f"neighbor_price_proxy - train target correlation: {corr_proxy:.4f}")
print(f"  (deliberate appraisal-style feature; KFold prevents leakage)")

neighbor_price_proxy - train target correlation: 0.6931
  (deliberate appraisal-style feature; KFold prevents leakage)

KNN neighbor price proxy: for each house, the predicted price from its 10 nearest geographic neighbors (by city/zip signal) is used as a spatial appraisal signal. KMeans cluster label groups properties into 12 price-based geographic zones.

The feature neighbor_price_proxy shows a strong correlation with the target (~0.69), indicating high predictive relevance consistent with an appraisal-based spatial effect. Despite its strength, the feature is not considered to introduce leakage due to its construction using KFold encoding, which ensures that target information from the same samples is not directly used during feature generation. Therefore, it remains a valid and informative feature for modeling.

In [23]:
# Truncated Power Spline Basis

sqft_train = df.iloc[train_idx]['sqft_living'].values
knot_pcts  = [10, 25, 40, 55, 70, 85]           
inner_knots= np.percentile(sqft_train, knot_pcts)

def truncated_power_basis(x, knots, degree=3):
    cols = [x**d for d in range(degree + 1)]     # 1, x, x², x³
    for k in knots:
        cols.append(np.maximum(x - k, 0) ** degree)
    return np.column_stack(cols)

spline_mat = truncated_power_basis(df['sqft_living'].values, inner_knots)
spline_cols = [f'spline_{i}' for i in range(spline_mat.shape[1])]
for i, col in enumerate(spline_cols):
    df[col] = spline_mat[:, i]

print(f"Spline basis: {len(spline_cols)} columns")
print(f"Knots at sqft_living percentiles {knot_pcts}: {inner_knots.astype(int).tolist()}")
print(f"\nTotal df columns: {df.shape[1]}")

Spline basis: 10 columns
Knots at sqft_living percentiles [10, 25, 40, 55, 70, 85]: [1100, 1460, 1770, 2070, 2470, 3010]

Total df columns: 59

Truncated power spline basis on sqft_living adds 10 basis columns that allow the model to fit smooth, flexible non-linear curves between size and price — without requiring a tree-based model. Note: this uses a truncated power basis, not a natural cubic spline; boundary behaviour remains cubic rather than linear beyond the outermost knots.

In [24]:
# Natural Cubic Spline Basis (patsy cr())
from patsy import dmatrix

ncs_train = dmatrix("cr(sqft_living, df=8) - 1",
                    {"sqft_living": df.iloc[train_idx]['sqft_living'].values},
                    return_type='dataframe')

ncs_full = dmatrix(ncs_train.design_info,
                   {"sqft_living": df['sqft_living'].values},
                   return_type='dataframe')

ncs_cols = [f'ncs_{i}' for i in range(ncs_full.shape[1])]
for i, col in enumerate(ncs_cols):
    df[col] = ncs_full.iloc[:, i].values

print(f"NCS basis: {len(ncs_cols)} columns (df=8)")

NCS basis: 8 columns (df=8)

Natural Cubic Spline basis using patsy cr() with df=8 (6 inner knots). Unlike the truncated power basis above, NCS enforces linear boundary conditions beyond the outermost knots, giving better extrapolation behaviour. Knot positions are fitted on the training set only; the same design is applied to test via design_info to prevent leakage.

In [25]:
# Encode city/zipcode as integers for tree model
df['city']    = le.fit_transform(df['city'].astype(str))
df['zipcode'] = le.fit_transform(df['zipcode'].astype(str))

# Define feature sets
core_features = [
    # Baseline (12)
    'bedrooms', 'bathrooms', 'sqft_living', 'sqft_lot', 'floors',
    'waterfront', 'view', 'condition', 'sqft_above', 'sqft_basement',
    'house_age', 'years_since_renovation',
    # Non-linear (8)
    'sqft_living_sq', 'sqft_living_sqrt', 'sqft_living_log',
    'sqft_lot_log', 'sqft_above_log', 'sqft_bsmt_log',
    'bath_per_bed', 'bed_bath_sum',
    # Domain (7)
    'lot_utilization', 'avg_room_size', 'has_basement',
    'above_grade_frac', 'basement_frac', 'effective_living', 'sqft_per_room',
    # Categorical (7)
    'is_luxury', 'is_renovated', 'is_historic', 'is_new',
    'age_category', 'waterfront_view', 'condition_view',
    # Interactions (7)
    'living_x_cond', 'living_x_view', 'living_x_wf',
    'log_living_x_cond', 'log_living_x_view', 'age_x_cond', 'bed_x_sqft',
    # Spatial (4)
    'city_price_signal', 'zipcode_price_signal',
    'neighbor_price_proxy', 'cluster_label',
]

extended_features = core_features + spline_cols  # adds 10 spline cols

X_train = df.loc[train_idx, core_features].copy()
X_test  = df.loc[test_idx,  core_features].copy()

print(f"CORE features    : {len(core_features)}")
print(f"EXTENDED features: {len(extended_features)}")
print(f"X_train: {X_train.shape}  |  X_test: {X_test.shape}")

CORE features    : 45
EXTENDED features: 55
X_train: (3629, 45)  |  X_test: (908, 45)

Two feature sets are defined: core (45 features, no splines) used for all linear and regularised models; extended (45 + 10 truncated power spline columns) used for Spline+Lasso and Spline+ElasticNet models; and ncs (45 + 8 NCS columns) used for the NCS+ElasticNet model in Section 12.

In [26]:
# Feature correlation with log_price
feat_corr = {}
for col in core_features:
    feat_corr[col] = abs(np.corrcoef(df.loc[train_idx, col], log_price_all.iloc[train_idx])[0, 1])

corr_series = pd.Series(feat_corr).sort_values(ascending=False)
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 6))
corr_series.plot(kind='bar', color='steelblue', edgecolor='white')
plt.axhline(0.3, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5, label='r = 0.30 threshold')
plt.title('Absolute Correlation of Each Feature with log_price (train set)', fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel('|Pearson r|')
plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha='right', fontsize=8)
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

print(f"\nTop 10 features by correlation with log_price:")
print(corr_series.head(10).to_string())

Top 10 features by correlation with log_price:
zipcode_price_signal   0.7066
neighbor_price_proxy   0.6931
sqft_living_sqrt       0.6847
sqft_living            0.6815
sqft_living_log        0.6715
effective_living       0.6601
living_x_cond          0.6556
sqft_living_sq         0.6147
city_price_signal      0.6016
sqft_above             0.5914

The chart highlights the top predictors for log_price, leading to the following observations:

  • Spatial Dominance: Location-based signals—specifically zipcode_price_signal (0.71) and neighbor_price_proxy (0.69)—show the strongest correlation with property value.
  • Structural Size: Features related to living area, such as sqft_living and its transformations, remain primary drivers of price.

7. Baseline OLS¶

In [27]:
# Evaluation function 
def mape(y_true_log, y_pred_log):
    return float(np.mean(np.abs(
        (np.expm1(y_true_log) - np.expm1(y_pred_log)) / np.expm1(y_true_log)
    )) * 100)

mape_scorer = make_scorer(mape, greater_is_better=False)
KF = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
results = {}

def evaluate(model, X_tr, y_tr, X_te, y_te, label, focus=''):
    model.fit(X_tr, y_tr)
    y_pred    = model.predict(X_te)
    y_pred_tr = model.predict(X_tr)
    t_mape    = mape(y_te, y_pred)
    tr_mape   = mape(y_tr, y_pred_tr)
    t_r2      = r2_score(y_te, y_pred)
    cv_raw    = cross_val_score(model, X_tr, y_tr, cv=KF,
                                scoring=mape_scorer, n_jobs=-1)
    cv_m, cv_s = float(-cv_raw.mean()), float(cv_raw.std())
    print(f"[{label}]  Train MAPE: {tr_mape:.2f}%  Test MAPE: {t_mape:.2f}%  R²: {t_r2:.4f}  |  CV MAPE: {cv_m:.2f}% ± {cv_s:.2f}%")
    return dict(label=label, model=model, train_mape=tr_mape, test_mape=t_mape, test_r2=t_r2,
                cv_mape=cv_m, cv_mape_std=cv_s, y_pred=y_pred, focus=focus)

print("evaluation function defined.")

evaluation function defined.
In [28]:
# Baseline OLS — no feature engineering, label-encoded city/zipcode
results['00_Baseline'] = evaluate(
    LinearRegression(),
    X_tr_base, y_train, X_te_base, y_test,
    '00. Baseline OLS', 'Raw data only (label encoded)'
)

[00. Baseline OLS]  Train MAPE: 30.40%  Test MAPE: 30.59%  R²: 0.4796  |  CV MAPE: 30.59% ± 1.80%

The baseline OLS — using raw features with label-encoded city/zip — defines our performance floor for evaluating feature engineering improvements. However, because label encoding imposes an artificial ordinal structure on locations, the model misinterprets these values and fails to capture key price signals, resulting in high MAPE.

8. Iterative OLS — Feature Engineering Stages¶

In [29]:
# 1: Polynomial + basic interactions
S1 = ['bedrooms','bathrooms','sqft_living','sqft_lot','floors',
      'waterfront','view','condition','sqft_above','sqft_basement',
      'house_age','years_since_renovation',
      'sqft_living_sq','sqft_living_log','sqft_lot_log','bath_per_bed',
      'living_x_cond','is_luxury']
results['01_Poly'] = evaluate(
    LinearRegression(), X_train[S1], y_train, X_test[S1], y_test,
    '01. OLS + Polynomial/Interaction', 'Poly terms + interactions')

[01. OLS + Polynomial/Interaction]  Train MAPE: 30.48%  Test MAPE: 28.67%  R²: 0.5112  |  CV MAPE: 30.72% ± 1.32%

Stage 1 - OLS + Polynomial & Interaction Terms introduces polynomial and interaction terms on top of the baseline, resulting in only a modest improvement. While features such as sqft_living², bath_per_bed, and living × condition add some nonlinearity, their impact remains limited. This highlights that structural attributes alone (e.g., size, number of rooms, condition) are not sufficient to explain price variation. Without any representation of location, the model still lacks critical spatial context. As a result, increasing feature complexity through polynomial terms cannot compensate for the missing location signal, leading to only marginal performance gains.

In [30]:
# 2: + Location signals (target encoding)
S2 = S1 + ['city_price_signal','zipcode_price_signal','age_category']
results['02_Location'] = evaluate(
    LinearRegression(), X_train[S2], y_train, X_test[S2], y_test,
    '02. OLS + Location Signals', 'KFold target encoding city/zip')

[02. OLS + Location Signals]  Train MAPE: 18.44%  Test MAPE: 18.93%  R²: 0.6862  |  CV MAPE: 18.70% ± 1.25%

Stage 2 — OLS + Location Signals introduces KFold target-encoded city and zip, delivering the largest MAPE drop in the entire experiment. This confirms that location is the dominant price driver. Unlike naive label encoding (Stage 0), which imposed false ordinal relationships and degraded performance, target encoding captures meaningful location signals.

In [31]:
# 3: + Domain ratios & indicators
S3 = S2 + ['lot_utilization','avg_room_size','has_basement','is_renovated',
            'is_historic','above_grade_frac','effective_living',
            'basement_frac','waterfront_view','condition_view']
results['03_Domain'] = evaluate(
    LinearRegression(), X_train[S3], y_train, X_test[S3], y_test,
    '03. OLS + Domain Ratios', 'Domain metrics + binary indicators')

[03. OLS + Domain Ratios]  Train MAPE: 18.23%  Test MAPE: 18.91%  R²: 0.6881  |  CV MAPE: 18.60% ± 1.31%

Stage 3 — OLS + Domain Ratios & Binary Flags adds engineered ratios and indicators, yielding moderate improvement. Features like lot_utilization and avg_room_size provide useful structural signals, but several binary flags (e.g., is_historic, is_new) add little value. Redundancy among features (e.g., bedrooms vs. derived ratios) introduces collinearity, limiting overall gains.

In [32]:
# 4: + KNN spatial + KMeans + extended interactions
S4 = S3 + ['neighbor_price_proxy','cluster_label',
            'sqft_living_sqrt','sqft_above_log','sqft_bsmt_log',
            'living_x_view','living_x_wf','log_living_x_cond',
            'log_living_x_view','age_x_cond','bed_x_sqft',
            'is_new','bed_bath_sum','sqft_per_room']
results['04_Spatial'] = evaluate(
    LinearRegression(), X_train[S4], y_train, X_test[S4], y_test,
    '04. OLS + KNN Spatial + Extended', 'KNN proxy + KMeans + all interactions')

[04. OLS + KNN Spatial + Extended]  Train MAPE: 17.65%  Test MAPE: 18.23%  R²: 0.6955  |  CV MAPE: 18.12% ± 0.99%

Stage 4 — OLS + Spatial Proxy & Clusters incorporates a KNN-based neighbor price proxy and KMeans cluster labels, delivering a solid improvement. The spatial proxy adds meaningful signal by capturing local price patterns, while the cluster label contributes limited standalone value and mainly serves as contextual grouping. Its ordinal encoding (0–11) also limits its effectiveness.

In [33]:
# 5: Full core feature set
results['05_Full_OLS'] = evaluate(
    LinearRegression(), X_train[core_features], y_train, X_test[core_features], y_test,
    '05. OLS Full Core (45 features)', 'All 45 core features')

[05. OLS Full Core (45 features)]  Train MAPE: 17.65%  Test MAPE: 18.23%  R²: 0.6955  |  CV MAPE: 18.12% ± 0.99%

Stage 5 — Full OLS (45 Features) produces identical results to Stage 4, confirming that the additional features introduced in Stage 4 already cover the full core feature set. Without regularisation, OLS remains susceptible to overfitting as features increase, motivating the transition to regularized models in the next section.

9. Regularized Models¶

In [34]:
# Ridge with alpha tuning
alphas_ridge = [0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 50.0, 100.0]
gs_ridge = GridSearchCV(
    Pipeline([('sc', RobustScaler()), ('m', Ridge())]),
    {'m__alpha': alphas_ridge}, cv=KF, scoring=mape_scorer, n_jobs=-1
)
gs_ridge.fit(X_train[core_features], y_train)
print(f"Best Ridge alpha: {gs_ridge.best_params_}")
results['06_Ridge'] = evaluate(
    gs_ridge.best_estimator_, X_train[core_features], y_train, X_test[core_features], y_test,
    '06. Ridge (L2 tuned)', 'L2 regularisation, tuned alpha')

Best Ridge alpha: {'m__alpha': 1.0}
[06. Ridge (L2 tuned)]  Train MAPE: 17.68%  Test MAPE: 18.26%  R²: 0.6948  |  CV MAPE: 18.06% ± 1.03%

Ridge (L2, tuned α) shrinks coefficients to reduce overfitting, producing stable generalisation comparable to full OLS. However, it retains all 45 features, including weak ones, limiting interpretability. Ridge also struggles slightly with highly collinear features (e.g., polynomial size terms), where ElasticNet's combined L1+L2 penalty provides marginally more stable estimates.

In [35]:
# Lasso with alpha tuning
alphas_lasso = [0.00005, 0.0001, 0.0005, 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05]
gs_lasso = GridSearchCV(
    Pipeline([('sc', RobustScaler()), ('m', Lasso(max_iter=10000))]),
    {'m__alpha': alphas_lasso}, cv=KF, scoring=mape_scorer, n_jobs=-1
)
gs_lasso.fit(X_train[core_features], y_train)
print(f"Best Lasso alpha: {gs_lasso.best_params_}")
results['07_Lasso'] = evaluate(
    gs_lasso.best_estimator_, X_train[core_features], y_train, X_test[core_features], y_test,
    '07. Lasso (L1 tuned)', 'L1 feature selection, tuned alpha')

Best Lasso alpha: {'m__alpha': 0.0001}
[07. Lasso (L1 tuned)]  Train MAPE: 17.71%  Test MAPE: 18.24%  R²: 0.6951  |  CV MAPE: 18.07% ± 1.04%

Lasso (L1, tuned α) performs automatic feature selection by shrinking some coefficients to zero, improving interpretability. However, performance gains are limited, and in the presence of correlated features, Lasso can be unstable—arbitrarily selecting one while discarding others—making results sensitive to data variation. This motivates the use of ElasticNet for greater stability.

In [36]:
# ElasticNet with grid search
param_en = {
    'm__alpha'   : [0.00005, 0.0001, 0.0005, 0.001, 0.005, 0.01],
    'm__l1_ratio': [0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9],
}
gs_en = GridSearchCV(
    Pipeline([('sc', RobustScaler()), ('m', ElasticNet(max_iter=10000))]),
    param_en, cv=KF, scoring=mape_scorer, n_jobs=-1, verbose=0
)
gs_en.fit(X_train[core_features], y_train)
best_en_params = gs_en.best_params_
print(f"Best ElasticNet: {best_en_params}")
results['08_ElasticNet'] = evaluate(
    gs_en.best_estimator_, X_train[core_features], y_train, X_test[core_features], y_test,
    '08. ElasticNet (tuned)', 'L1+L2, grid-searched alpha & l1_ratio')

Best ElasticNet: {'m__alpha': 0.0005, 'm__l1_ratio': 0.1}
[08. ElasticNet (tuned)]  Train MAPE: 17.70%  Test MAPE: 18.25%  R²: 0.6951  |  CV MAPE: 18.07% ± 1.05%

ElasticNet (L1 + L2, tuned α & l1_ratio) combines sparsity and shrinkage, making it among the most stable regularised linear models in this experiment. It outperforms Lasso in handling correlated features while still performing feature selection. However, performance is sensitive to l1_ratio: overly L1-heavy settings lead to aggressive feature removal and worse results, while a balanced mix of L1 and L2 produces the best generalisation.

In [37]:
# Huber Regressor with grid search
param_huber = {'m__epsilon': [1.1, 1.2, 1.35, 1.5, 1.75, 2.0],
               'm__alpha'  : [0.0001, 0.001, 0.01]}
gs_huber = GridSearchCV(
    Pipeline([('sc', RobustScaler()), ('m', HuberRegressor(max_iter=5000))]),
    param_huber, cv=KF, scoring=mape_scorer, n_jobs=-1
)
gs_huber.fit(X_train[core_features], y_train)
print(f"Best Huber params: {gs_huber.best_params_}")
results['09_Huber'] = evaluate(
    gs_huber.best_estimator_, X_train[core_features], y_train, X_test[core_features], y_test,
    '09. Huber (tuned)', 'Robust to outliers, tuned epsilon')

Best Huber params: {'m__alpha': 0.0001, 'm__epsilon': 2.0}
[09. Huber (tuned)]  Train MAPE: 19.36%  Test MAPE: 19.44%  R²: 0.5746  |  CV MAPE: 19.69% ± 0.72%

Huber Regressor (tuned α & ε) applies a robust loss that is quadratic for small errors and linear for large ones, making it less sensitive to outliers. However, it underperforms all other regularised models in this setting. This is likely because the log-transformation of price already reduces the influence of extreme values, diminishing Huber's robustness advantage. As a result, Huber increases computational cost (slower convergence) without meaningful MAPE improvement.

In [38]:
# Bayesian Ridge
results['10_BayesianRidge'] = evaluate(
    Pipeline([('sc', RobustScaler()), ('m', BayesianRidge(max_iter=500))]),
    X_train[core_features], y_train, X_test[core_features], y_test,
    '10. Bayesian Ridge', 'Automatic regularisation via marginal likelihood')

[10. Bayesian Ridge]  Train MAPE: 17.72%  Test MAPE: 18.23%  R²: 0.6957  |  CV MAPE: 18.08% ± 1.07%

Bayesian Ridge infers optimal regularisation strength via marginal likelihood, removing the need for manual tuning. However, in this experiment it achieves only marginal performance, converging to results similar to tuned Ridge without meaningful MAPE improvement. While it offers the additional benefit of posterior uncertainty estimates, this advantage is not utilised here since evaluation is based on point predictions. Overall, it is not preferred over tuned Ridge or ElasticNet for pure MAPE optimisation.

10. Advanced Linear Models¶

  • Ridge + Polynomial features (degree-2 PolynomialFeatures on top spatial features)
  • Quantile Regression (predict median instead of mean — robust to skew)
  • Feature-selected ElasticNet (RFECV to remove weak features)
  • PCA-compressed Ridge (dimensionality reduction before Ridge)
In [39]:
# Ridge + Polynomial Features (degree=2 on selected features)
class PolySubsetRidge:
    def __init__(self, poly_cols, remaining_cols, degree=2, alpha=10.0):
        self.poly_cols = poly_cols
        self.remaining_cols = remaining_cols

        self.poly = PolynomialFeatures(degree=degree, include_bias=False)
        self.sc_poly = RobustScaler()
        self.sc_rest = RobustScaler()
        self.model = Ridge(alpha=alpha)

    def fit(self, X, y):
        Xp = self.sc_poly.fit_transform(X[self.poly_cols].values)
        Xp = self.poly.fit_transform(Xp)

        Xr = self.sc_rest.fit_transform(X[self.remaining_cols].values)

        Xc = np.hstack([Xp, Xr])

        self.model.fit(Xc, y)
        return self

    def predict(self, X):
        Xp = self.sc_poly.transform(X[self.poly_cols].values)
        Xp = self.poly.transform(Xp)

        Xr = self.sc_rest.transform(X[self.remaining_cols].values)

        return self.model.predict(np.hstack([Xp, Xr]))


poly_cols = ['sqft_living_log', 'city_price_signal', 'zipcode_price_signal',
             'neighbor_price_proxy', 'condition', 'view', 'house_age']
remaining_core = [col for col in core_features if col not in poly_cols]

# Tune alpha
best_poly_alpha, best_poly_mape = 10.0, np.inf
for alpha in [1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 50.0, 100.0]:
    cv_m = []
    for tr_r, va_r in KF.split(X_train):
        m = PolySubsetRidge(poly_cols, remaining_core, degree=2, alpha=alpha)
        m.fit(X_train.iloc[tr_r], y_train.iloc[tr_r])
        cv_m.append(mape(y_train.iloc[va_r], m.predict(X_train.iloc[va_r])))
    if np.mean(cv_m) < best_poly_mape:
        best_poly_mape = np.mean(cv_m)
        best_poly_alpha = alpha

poly_model = PolySubsetRidge(
    poly_cols=poly_cols,
    remaining_cols=remaining_core,
    degree=2,
    alpha=best_poly_alpha
)

poly_model.fit(X_train, y_train)

y_pred_train = poly_model.predict(X_train)
y_pred_test  = poly_model.predict(X_test)

poly_train_mape = mape(y_train, y_pred_train)
poly_test_mape  = mape(y_test, y_pred_test)
poly_r2         = r2_score(y_test, y_pred_test)

cv_poly = []

X_tr = X_train.reset_index(drop=True)
y_tr = y_train.reset_index(drop=True)

for tr_idx, va_idx in KF.split(X_tr):

    m = PolySubsetRidge(
        poly_cols=poly_cols,
        remaining_cols=remaining_core,
        degree=2,
        alpha=best_poly_alpha
    )

    m.fit(X_tr.iloc[tr_idx], y_tr.iloc[tr_idx])
    pred_va = m.predict(X_tr.iloc[va_idx])

    cv_poly.append(mape(y_tr.iloc[va_idx], pred_va))

cv_poly_mean = np.mean(cv_poly)
cv_poly_std  = np.std(cv_poly)

results['11_PolyRidge'] = dict(
    model=poly_model,
    label='11. Ridge Regression with Polynomial Interaction Features',
    train_mape=poly_train_mape,
    test_mape=poly_test_mape,
    test_r2=poly_r2,
    cv_mape=cv_poly_mean,
    cv_mape_std=cv_poly_std,
    focus=f'Degree-2 poly + RobustScaler + Ridge, alpha={best_poly_alpha}'
)

print(
    f"[11. PolyRidge] "
    f"Train MAPE: {poly_train_mape:.2f}% | "
    f"Test MAPE: {poly_test_mape:.2f}% | "
    f"R²: {poly_r2:.4f} | "
    f"CV MAPE: {cv_poly_mean:.2f}% ± {cv_poly_std:.2f}%"
)

[11. PolyRidge] Train MAPE: 17.31% | Test MAPE: 17.91% | R²: 0.6981 | CV MAPE: 17.90% ± 1.14%

PolyRidge — Ridge + Degree-2 Polynomial Features is the most flexible linear model in this experiment. By applying polynomial expansion to key spatial and structural features, it captures non-linear interactions (e.g. sqft_living² × condition) that standard linear models cannot represent.

This setup achieves the best performance among linear approaches, striking a balance between expressiveness and generalisation. A degree-3 expansion was also tested but led to clear overfitting due to the high-dimensional feature space, which even Ridge could not sufficiently regularise. Degree-2 therefore represents the optimal trade-off between model complexity and stability.

In [40]:
# Quantile Regression (predict median, robust to outliers)
# Uses quantile=0.5 (median regression) -> less sensitive to luxury outliers
# Train Quantile model
sc_q = RobustScaler()
X_tr_sc_q = sc_q.fit_transform(X_train[core_features])
X_te_sc_q = sc_q.transform(X_test[core_features])

best_q_alpha, best_q_mape = 0.001, np.inf

for alpha in [0.0001, 0.001, 0.01, 0.1]:
    cv_m = []
    
    for tr_r, va_r in KF.split(X_tr_sc_q):
        qr = QuantileRegressor(quantile=0.5, alpha=alpha, solver='highs')
        qr.fit(X_tr_sc_q[tr_r], y_train.values[tr_r])
        
        pred_va = qr.predict(X_tr_sc_q[va_r])
        cv_m.append(mape(y_train.values[va_r], pred_va))
    
    if np.mean(cv_m) < best_q_mape:
        best_q_mape = np.mean(cv_m)
        best_q_alpha = alpha

# Fit best model
qr_best = QuantileRegressor(
    quantile=0.5,
    alpha=best_q_alpha,
    solver='highs'
)

qr_best.fit(X_tr_sc_q, y_train)

y_pred_train_qr = qr_best.predict(X_tr_sc_q)
y_pred_test_qr  = qr_best.predict(X_te_sc_q)

qr_train_mape = mape(y_train, y_pred_train_qr)
qr_test_mape  = mape(y_test, y_pred_test_qr)
qr_r2         = r2_score(y_test, y_pred_test_qr)

cv_qr = []

for tr_r, va_r in KF.split(X_tr_sc_q):
    qr = QuantileRegressor(
        quantile=0.5,
        alpha=best_q_alpha,
        solver='highs'
    )
    
    qr.fit(X_tr_sc_q[tr_r], y_train.values[tr_r])
    
    pred_va = qr.predict(X_tr_sc_q[va_r])
    cv_qr.append(mape(y_train.values[va_r], pred_va))

cv_qr_mean = np.mean(cv_qr)
cv_qr_std  = np.std(cv_qr)

results['12_Quantile'] = dict(
    label='12. Quantile Regression (median)',
    train_mape=qr_train_mape,
    test_mape=qr_test_mape,
    test_r2=qr_r2,
    cv_mape=cv_qr_mean,
    cv_mape_std=cv_qr_std,
    focus='Median regression, robust to price outliers'
)

print(f"[12. Quantile Regression] "
      f"Train MAPE: {qr_train_mape:.2f}% | "
      f"Test MAPE: {qr_test_mape:.2f}% | "
      f"R²: {qr_r2:.4f} | "
      f"CV MAPE: {cv_qr_mean:.2f}% ± {cv_qr_std:.2f}%")

[12. Quantile Regression] Train MAPE: 17.80% | Test MAPE: 18.31% | R²: 0.6961 | CV MAPE: 18.26% ± 1.06%

Quantile Regression (median, tuned α) minimises absolute error rather than squared error, making it inherently robust to outliers. However, it does not outperform standard OLS in this setting. This is because the log-transform of price already stabilises and symmetrises the target distribution, reducing the need for median-based robustness. As a result, quantile regression provides no additional MAPE benefit while being computationally slower due to its linear programming optimisation.

In [41]:
# ElasticNet with Recursive Feature Elimination (RFECV)
sc_rfe = RobustScaler()
X_tr_sc_rfe = sc_rfe.fit_transform(X_train[core_features])
X_te_sc_rfe = sc_rfe.transform(X_test[core_features])

en_params = {k.replace('m__',''): v for k, v in best_en_params.items()}
base_en = ElasticNet(**en_params, max_iter=10000)

selector = RFECV(
    estimator=base_en,
    step=1,
    cv=KF,
    scoring=mape_scorer,
    min_features_to_select=10,
    n_jobs=-1
)

selector.fit(X_tr_sc_rfe, y_train)

selected_features = [
    core_features[i]
    for i in range(len(core_features))
    if selector.support_[i]
]

print(f"RFECV selected {len(selected_features)} / {len(core_features)} features")

en_rfe = ElasticNet(**en_params, max_iter=10000)

X_train_sel = X_tr_sc_rfe[:, selector.support_]
X_test_sel  = X_te_sc_rfe[:, selector.support_]

en_rfe.fit(X_train_sel, y_train)

y_pred_train = en_rfe.predict(X_train_sel)
y_pred_test  = en_rfe.predict(X_test_sel)

train_mape = mape(y_train, y_pred_train)
test_mape  = mape(y_test, y_pred_test)
test_r2    = r2_score(y_test, y_pred_test)

cv_en = []

for tr_r, va_r in KF.split(X_tr_sc_rfe):
    X_tr_cv = X_tr_sc_rfe[tr_r][:, selector.support_]
    X_va_cv = X_tr_sc_rfe[va_r][:, selector.support_]

    m = ElasticNet(**en_params, max_iter=10000)
    m.fit(X_tr_cv, y_train.values[tr_r])

    pred_va = m.predict(X_va_cv)
    cv_en.append(mape(y_train.values[va_r], pred_va))

cv_mape = np.mean(cv_en)
cv_std  = np.std(cv_en)

results['13_RFECV_EN'] = dict(
    label='13. ElasticNet + RFECV',
    train_mape=train_mape,
    test_mape=test_mape,
    test_r2=test_r2,
    cv_mape=cv_mape,
    cv_mape_std=cv_std,
    focus=f'RFECV selected {len(selected_features)} features'
)

print(f"[13. ElasticNet + RFECV] "
      f"Train MAPE: {train_mape:.2f}% | "
      f"Test MAPE: {test_mape:.2f}% | "
      f"R²: {test_r2:.4f} | "
      f"CV MAPE: {cv_mape:.2f}% ± {cv_std:.2f}%")

RFECV selected 35 / 45 features
[13. ElasticNet + RFECV] Train MAPE: 17.72% | Test MAPE: 18.28% | R²: 0.6950 | CV MAPE: 18.04% ± 1.07%

ElasticNet + RFECV (recursive feature elimination) uses cross-validated MAPE to iteratively remove the weakest features and identify the most predictive subset.

However, feature reduction does not improve performance in this case. The selected subset achieves similar MAPE to the full ElasticNet model, suggesting that many weak features still contribute small but cumulative predictive signal. In addition, RFECV is computationally expensive due to repeated cross-validation across elimination steps, making it inefficient for rapid experimentation. Overall, it does not provide a practical advantage over the full ElasticNet pipeline.

In [42]:
# PCA + Ridge

best_pca_alpha = 1.0
best_pca_m = np.inf

for alpha in [0.1, 1.0, 10.0, 50.0, 100.0]:
    pipe = Pipeline([
        ('sc', RobustScaler()),
        ('pca', PCA(n_components=0.95, random_state=42)),
        ('m', Ridge(alpha=alpha))
    ])
    
    cv_s = -cross_val_score(
        pipe,
        X_train[core_features],
        y_train,
        cv=KF,
        scoring=mape_scorer,
        n_jobs=-1
    )
    
    if cv_s.mean() < best_pca_m:
        best_pca_m = cv_s.mean()
        best_pca_alpha = alpha

pca_final = Pipeline([
    ('sc', RobustScaler()),
    ('pca', PCA(n_components=0.95, random_state=42)),
    ('m', Ridge(alpha=best_pca_alpha))
])

pca_final.fit(X_train[core_features], y_train)

y_pred_train = pca_final.predict(X_train[core_features])
y_pred_test  = pca_final.predict(X_test[core_features])

train_mape = mape(y_train, y_pred_train)
test_mape  = mape(y_test, y_pred_test)
test_r2    = r2_score(y_test, y_pred_test)

cv_pca = []

for tr_r, va_r in KF.split(X_train):
    pipe = Pipeline([
        ('sc', RobustScaler()),
        ('pca', PCA(n_components=0.95, random_state=42)),
        ('m', Ridge(alpha=best_pca_alpha))
    ])
    
    pipe.fit(X_train.iloc[tr_r][core_features], y_train.iloc[tr_r])
    
    pred_va = pipe.predict(X_train.iloc[va_r][core_features])
    cv_pca.append(mape(y_train.iloc[va_r], pred_va))

cv_mean = np.mean(cv_pca)
cv_std  = np.std(cv_pca)

results['14_PCA_Ridge'] = dict(
    label='14. PCA + Ridge',
    train_mape=train_mape,
    test_mape=test_mape,
    test_r2=test_r2,
    cv_mape=cv_mean,
    cv_mape_std=cv_std,
    focus=f'PCA 95% variance + Ridge alpha={best_pca_alpha}'
)

print(f"[14. PCA + Ridge] "
      f"Train MAPE: {train_mape:.2f}% | "
      f"Test MAPE: {test_mape:.2f}% | "
      f"R²: {test_r2:.4f} | "
      f"CV MAPE: {cv_mean:.2f}% ± {cv_std:.2f}%")

[14. PCA + Ridge] Train MAPE: 43.95% | Test MAPE: 42.13% | R²: 0.0915 | CV MAPE: 43.98% ± 3.69%

PCA + Ridge reduces the 45 engineered features into principal components retaining 95% of variance, followed by Ridge regression as a baseline linear model.

This is the worst-performing advanced approach in the experiment. While PCA reduces dimensionality, it also destroys interpretability and mixes meaningful signals (e.g. spatial and structural features) into abstract components. As a result, predictive signal is lost rather than noise, leading to higher MAPE. PCA is generally more suitable for very high-dimensional settings, and in this case it is unnecessary given the relatively small, well-engineered feature set.

11. Ensemble Models¶

Three ensemble strategies:

  1. Simple average blend — equal-weight average of best linear models
  2. Optimally weighted blend — weights found by minimising CV MAPE
  3. Stacking (meta-learner) — ElasticNet meta-learner trained on OOF predictions

Out-of-fold (OOF) prediction is a prediction made for each data point using a model that was never trained on that specific data point.

In [43]:
# compute OOF (out-of-fold) predictions
def get_oof_predictions(model_pipe, X_tr, y_tr, X_te, kf):
    oof   = np.zeros(len(X_tr))
    te_preds = np.zeros((len(X_te), kf.get_n_splits()))

    for fold_i, (tr_idx, va_idx) in enumerate(kf.split(X_tr)):
        import copy
        m = copy.deepcopy(model_pipe)
        m.fit(X_tr.iloc[tr_idx], y_tr.iloc[tr_idx])
        oof[va_idx]          = m.predict(X_tr.iloc[va_idx])
        te_preds[:, fold_i]  = m.predict(X_te)

    return oof, te_preds.mean(axis=1)

print("oof_predictions() defined.")

oof_predictions() defined.
In [44]:
# Base model pipelines for ensemble stacking
en_params_clean = {k.replace('m__',''): v for k, v in best_en_params.items()}
ridge_params_clean = {k.replace('m__',''): v for k, v in gs_ridge.best_params_.items()}

base_pipes = {
    'ElasticNet': Pipeline([('sc', RobustScaler()),
                             ('m', ElasticNet(**en_params_clean, max_iter=10000))]),
    'Lasso'     : Pipeline([('sc', RobustScaler()),
                             ('m', Lasso(**{k.replace('m__',''):v for k,v in gs_lasso.best_params_.items()}, max_iter=10000))]),
    'Ridge'     : Pipeline([('sc', RobustScaler()),
                             ('m', Ridge(**ridge_params_clean))]),
    'Huber'     : gs_huber.best_estimator_,
    'BayesRidge': Pipeline([('sc', RobustScaler()), ('m', BayesianRidge(max_iter=500))]),
}

# Get OOF + test predictions for each base model
oof_preds = {}
te_preds  = {}
for name, pipe in base_pipes.items():
    oof_preds[name], te_preds[name] = get_oof_predictions(
        pipe, X_train[core_features], y_train, X_test[core_features], KF)
    print(f"  OOF {name}: MAPE = {mape(y_train, oof_preds[name]):.2f}%")

  OOF ElasticNet: MAPE = 18.07%
  OOF Lasso: MAPE = 18.07%
  OOF Ridge: MAPE = 18.06%
  OOF Huber: MAPE = 19.69%
  OOF BayesRidge: MAPE = 18.08%

Out-of-fold predictions from each base model serve as meta-features. The OOF MAPE for each base model here reflects its standalone generalisation performance, input quality for the meta-learner.

In [45]:
# 1: Equal-weight blend

y_blend_eq = np.mean([te_preds[n] for n in te_preds], axis=0)

blend_mape = mape(y_test, y_blend_eq)
blend_r2   = r2_score(y_test, y_blend_eq)

oof_blend_eq = np.mean([oof_preds[n] for n in oof_preds], axis=0)

blend_train_mape = mape(y_train, oof_blend_eq)

cv_blend = []

for tr_r, va_r in KF.split(X_train[core_features]):
    
    blend_cv_pred = np.zeros(len(va_r))
    
    for name, pipe in base_pipes.items():
        m = copy.deepcopy(pipe)
        
        m.fit(X_train[core_features].iloc[tr_r], y_train.iloc[tr_r])
        blend_cv_pred += m.predict(X_train[core_features].iloc[va_r])
    
    blend_cv_pred /= len(base_pipes)
    
    cv_blend.append(
        mape(y_train.iloc[va_r], blend_cv_pred)
    )

cv_mean = np.mean(cv_blend)
cv_std  = np.std(cv_blend)

results['15_EqualBlend'] = dict(
    label='15. Equal-Weight Blend (5 models)',
    train_mape=blend_train_mape,
    test_mape=blend_mape,
    test_r2=blend_r2,
    cv_mape=cv_mean,
    cv_mape_std=cv_std,
    focus='Average of ElasticNet, Lasso, Ridge, Huber, BayesRidge'
)

print(f"[15. Equal-Weight Blend] "
      f"Train MAPE: {blend_train_mape:.2f}% | "
      f"Test MAPE: {blend_mape:.2f}% | "
      f"R²: {blend_r2:.4f} | "
      f"CV MAPE: {cv_mean:.2f}% ± {cv_std:.2f}%")

[15. Equal-Weight Blend] Train MAPE: 18.13% | Test MAPE: 18.28% | R²: 0.6917 | CV MAPE: 18.13% ± 0.99%

Equal-Weight Blend averages predictions from ElasticNet, Lasso, Ridge, Huber, and Bayesian Ridge to reduce variance through model diversity.

In practice, the improvement is marginal. Since all base models share the same linear structure and feature set, their predictions are highly correlated, limiting the benefit of ensembling. As a result, equal weighting slightly dilutes stronger models rather than meaningfully improving generalisation, yielding no meaningful gain over the best single regularised model.

In [46]:
# 2: Optimal-weight blend (non-negative least squares on OOF)

oof_matrix = np.column_stack([oof_preds[n] for n in oof_preds])
te_matrix  = np.column_stack([te_preds[n] for n in te_preds])

def blend_mape_loss(weights):
    w = np.abs(weights)
    w = w / w.sum()
    pred = oof_matrix @ w
    return mape(y_train, pred)

n_base = len(base_pipes)
w0 = np.ones(n_base) / n_base

result = minimize(
    blend_mape_loss,
    w0,
    method='SLSQP',
    bounds=[(0, 1)] * n_base,
    constraints={'type': 'eq', 'fun': lambda w: w.sum() - 1}
)

opt_weights = np.abs(result.x)
opt_weights = opt_weights / opt_weights.sum()

print("Optimal blend weights:")
for name, w in zip(base_pipes.keys(), opt_weights):
    print(f"  {name:12s}: {w:.4f}")

y_blend_opt = te_matrix @ opt_weights

opt_test_mape = mape(y_test, y_blend_opt)
opt_test_r2   = r2_score(y_test, y_blend_opt)

opt_train_mape = mape(y_train, oof_matrix @ opt_weights)

opt_cv_mape = opt_train_mape  # OOF already acts as CV estimate
opt_cv_std  = 0.0

results['16_OptBlend'] = dict(
    label='16. Optimal-Weight Blend',
    train_mape=opt_train_mape,
    test_mape=opt_test_mape,
    test_r2=opt_test_r2,
    cv_mape=opt_cv_mape,
    cv_mape_std=opt_cv_std,
    focus='SLSQP-optimised weights on OOF predictions'
)

print(f"[16. Optimal-Weight Blend] "
      f"Train MAPE: {opt_train_mape:.2f}% | "
      f"Test MAPE: {opt_test_mape:.2f}% | "
      f"R²: {opt_test_r2:.4f}")

Optimal blend weights:
  ElasticNet  : 0.0000
  Lasso       : 0.0000
  Ridge       : 0.8550
  Huber       : 0.0230
  BayesRidge  : 0.1220
[16. Optimal-Weight Blend] Train MAPE: 18.06% | Test MAPE: 18.25% | R²: 0.6950

Optimal-Weight Blend uses constrained optimisation on out-of-fold predictions to find model weights that minimise MAPE, rather than assigning equal weights.

It performs only marginally better than the equal-weight blend. The optimiser effectively assigns zero weight to ElasticNet and Lasso, concentrating most weight on Ridge, with minor contributions from BayesRidge and Huber. However, the learned weights do not fully generalise to the test set, as optimising on OOF predictions introduces its own form of overfitting to the validation distribution. Overall improvement remains within the noise margin compared to simpler averaging.

In [47]:
# 3: Stacking (ElasticNet meta-learner on OOF features)
# Meta-features: OOF predictions from each base model

# Meta features
meta_train = oof_matrix   # (n_train, n_models)
meta_test  = te_matrix    # (n_test, n_models)

# Tune meta model alpha
best_meta_alpha, best_meta_mape = 0.001, np.inf

for alpha in [0.0001, 0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1.0]:
    cv_m = []
    
    for tr_r, va_r in KF.split(meta_train):
        meta = ElasticNet(alpha=alpha, l1_ratio=0.5, max_iter=5000)
        meta.fit(meta_train[tr_r], y_train.values[tr_r])
        
        pred_va = meta.predict(meta_train[va_r])
        cv_m.append(mape(y_train.values[va_r], pred_va))
    
    if np.mean(cv_m) < best_meta_mape:
        best_meta_mape = np.mean(cv_m)
        best_meta_alpha = alpha

# meta learner
meta_learner = ElasticNet(
    alpha=best_meta_alpha,
    l1_ratio=0.5,
    max_iter=5000
)

meta_learner.fit(meta_train, y_train)

y_stack_train = meta_learner.predict(meta_train)
y_stack_test  = meta_learner.predict(meta_test)

stack_train_mape = mape(y_train, y_stack_train)
stack_test_mape  = mape(y_test, y_stack_test)
stack_test_r2    = r2_score(y_test, y_stack_test)

cv_stack = []

for tr_r, va_r in KF.split(meta_train):
    m = ElasticNet(
        alpha=best_meta_alpha,
        l1_ratio=0.5,
        max_iter=5000
    )
    
    m.fit(meta_train[tr_r], y_train.values[tr_r])
    
    pred_va = m.predict(meta_train[va_r])
    cv_stack.append(mape(y_train.values[va_r], pred_va))

cv_mean = np.mean(cv_stack)
cv_std  = np.std(cv_stack)

results['17_Stacking'] = dict(
    label='17. Stacking (ElasticNet meta)',
    train_mape=stack_train_mape,
    test_mape=stack_test_mape,
    test_r2=stack_test_r2,
    cv_mape=cv_mean,
    cv_mape_std=cv_std,
    focus='OOF stacking with ElasticNet meta-learner'
)

print(f"[17. Stacking] "
      f"Train MAPE: {stack_train_mape:.2f}% | "
      f"Test MAPE: {stack_test_mape:.2f}% | "
      f"R²: {stack_test_r2:.4f} | "
      f"CV MAPE: {cv_mean:.2f}% ± {cv_std:.2f}%")

[17. Stacking] Train MAPE: 18.03% | Test MAPE: 18.17% | R²: 0.6962 | CV MAPE: 18.08% ± 1.11%

Stacking (ElasticNet meta-learner on OOF) trains a second-level ElasticNet model on out-of-fold predictions from all base models, aim to learn optimal combinations of their outputs.

However, it does not outperform the best single model. The meta-learner primarily relies on Ridge, mirroring the behaviour observed in optimal blending.

12. Spline Models¶

In [48]:
# Spline + Lasso

X_train_ext = df.loc[train_idx, extended_features].copy()
X_test_ext  = df.loc[test_idx, extended_features].copy()

# Grid Search
alphas_spl = [0.00001, 0.00005, 0.0001, 0.0005, 0.001, 0.005]

gs_spl = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=Pipeline([
        ('sc', RobustScaler()),
        ('m', Lasso(max_iter=15000))
    ]),
    param_grid={'m__alpha': alphas_spl},
    cv=KF,
    scoring=mape_scorer,
    n_jobs=-1
)

gs_spl.fit(X_train_ext, y_train)

best_model = gs_spl.best_estimator_
best_alpha = gs_spl.best_params_['m__alpha']

print(f"Best Spline+Lasso alpha: {best_alpha}")

best_model.fit(X_train_ext, y_train)

y_pred_train = best_model.predict(X_train_ext)
y_pred_test  = best_model.predict(X_test_ext)

train_mape = mape(y_train, y_pred_train)
test_mape  = mape(y_test, y_pred_test)
test_r2    = r2_score(y_test, y_pred_test)

cv_scores = []

for tr_r, va_r in KF.split(X_train_ext):
    m = Pipeline([
        ('sc', RobustScaler()),
        ('m', Lasso(alpha=best_alpha, max_iter=15000))
    ])
    
    m.fit(X_train_ext.iloc[tr_r], y_train.iloc[tr_r])
    
    pred_va = m.predict(X_train_ext.iloc[va_r])
    cv_scores.append(mape(y_train.iloc[va_r], pred_va))

cv_mean = np.mean(cv_scores)
cv_std  = np.std(cv_scores)

results['18_Spline'] = dict(
    label='18. Spline + Lasso (tuned)',
    train_mape=train_mape,
    test_mape=test_mape,
    test_r2=test_r2,
    cv_mape=cv_mean,
    cv_mape_std=cv_std,
    focus='Truncated power basis, 6 inner knots'
)

print(f"[18. Spline + Lasso] "
      f"Train MAPE: {train_mape:.2f}% | "
      f"Test MAPE: {test_mape:.2f}% | "
      f"R²: {test_r2:.4f} | "
      f"CV MAPE: {cv_mean:.2f}% ± {cv_std:.2f}%")

Best Spline+Lasso alpha: 0.0001
[18. Spline + Lasso] Train MAPE: 17.71% | Test MAPE: 18.45% | R²: 0.6901 | CV MAPE: 18.09% ± 1.05%

Spline + Lasso extends the feature space with spline-transformed versions of key variables, allowing the model to capture smooth non-linear relationships, while Lasso performs automatic selection of the resulting basis functions.

The model does not improve over standard Lasso, with Test MAPE slightly higher. This is partly because splines are applied only to sqft_living, leaving other potentially non-linear variables (e.g. lot size, house age) untreated. In addition, Lasso eliminates several spline components, indicating that some knots do not align well with the underlying data structure.

In [49]:
# Spline + ElasticNet

X_train_ext = df.loc[train_idx, extended_features].copy()
X_test_ext  = df.loc[test_idx, extended_features].copy()

# Grid Search
alphas_spl = [0.00001, 0.00005, 0.0001, 0.0005, 0.001, 0.005]

gs_spl_en = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=Pipeline([
        ('sc', RobustScaler()),
        ('m', ElasticNet(max_iter=15000))
    ]),
    param_grid={
        'm__alpha': alphas_spl,
        'm__l1_ratio': [0.3, 0.5, 0.7]
    },
    cv=KF,
    scoring=mape_scorer,
    n_jobs=-1
)

gs_spl_en.fit(X_train_ext, y_train)

best_model = gs_spl_en.best_estimator_
best_params = gs_spl_en.best_params_

print(f"Best Spline+ElasticNet: {best_params}")

best_model.fit(X_train_ext, y_train)

y_pred_train = best_model.predict(X_train_ext)
y_pred_test  = best_model.predict(X_test_ext)

train_mape = mape(y_train, y_pred_train)
test_mape  = mape(y_test, y_pred_test)
test_r2    = r2_score(y_test, y_pred_test)

cv_scores = []

for tr_r, va_r in KF.split(X_train_ext):
    m = Pipeline([
        ('sc', RobustScaler()),
        ('m', ElasticNet(
            alpha=best_params['m__alpha'],
            l1_ratio=best_params['m__l1_ratio'],
            max_iter=15000
        ))
    ])
    
    m.fit(X_train_ext.iloc[tr_r], y_train.iloc[tr_r])
    
    pred_va = m.predict(X_train_ext.iloc[va_r])
    cv_scores.append(mape(y_train.iloc[va_r], pred_va))

cv_mean = np.mean(cv_scores)
cv_std  = np.std(cv_scores)

results['19_Spline_EN'] = dict(
    label='19. Spline + ElasticNet',
    train_mape=train_mape,
    test_mape=test_mape,
    test_r2=test_r2,
    cv_mape=cv_mean,
    cv_mape_std=cv_std,
    focus='Truncated power basis + L1+L2 regularization'
)

print(f"[19. Spline + ElasticNet] "
      f"Train MAPE: {train_mape:.2f}% | "
      f"Test MAPE: {test_mape:.2f}% | "
      f"R²: {test_r2:.4f} | "
      f"CV MAPE: {cv_mean:.2f}% ± {cv_std:.2f}%")

Best Spline+ElasticNet: {'m__alpha': 0.0005, 'm__l1_ratio': 0.3}
[19. Spline + ElasticNet] Train MAPE: 17.72% | Test MAPE: 18.43% | R²: 0.6909 | CV MAPE: 18.09% ± 1.06%

Spline + ElasticNet combines spline-expanded features with L1+L2 regularisation, with alpha and l1_ratio tuned via grid search.

It achieves performance similar to Spline + Lasso, with L2 providing slightly more stable coefficient estimates but no meaningful reduction in MAPE. Overall, this approach is outperformed by PolyRidge, which better captures cross-feature interactions (e.g. size × condition) rather than only modeling non-linearity within individual variables. This suggests that the dominant non-linearity in the data arises from feature interactions rather than single-variable curves.

In [ ]:
# NCS + ElasticNet
ncs_features = core_features + ncs_cols # adds 8 NCS cols

X_train_ncs = df.loc[train_idx, ncs_features].copy()
X_test_ncs  = df.loc[test_idx,  ncs_features].copy()

alphas_ncs = [0.00001, 0.00005, 0.0001, 0.0005, 0.001, 0.005]

gs_ncs = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=Pipeline([
        ('sc', RobustScaler()),
        ('m', ElasticNet(max_iter=15000))
    ]),
    param_grid={
        'm__alpha'   : alphas_ncs,
        'm__l1_ratio': [0.3, 0.5, 0.7]
    },
    cv=KF,
    scoring=mape_scorer,
    n_jobs=-1
)

gs_ncs.fit(X_train_ncs, y_train)
best_ncs = gs_ncs.best_estimator_
best_ncs.fit(X_train_ncs, y_train)

results['20_NCS_EN'] = evaluate(
    best_ncs, X_train_ncs, y_train, X_test_ncs, y_test,
    '20. NCS + ElasticNet', f'Natural Cubic Spline (df=8) + ElasticNet, params={gs_ncs.best_params_}'
)

[20. NCS + ElasticNet]  Train MAPE: 17.67%  Test MAPE: 18.25%  R²: 0.6948  |  CV MAPE: 18.09% ± 1.05%

NCS + ElasticNet replaces the truncated power basis with a proper Natural Cubic Spline expansion. Although NCS shows marginal improvement over the truncated power variants, the difference is negligible, confirming that boundary behaviour has limited impact on this dataset — a useful negative result demonstrating that spline type matters less than knot placement and regularisation strength.

13. XGBoost Benchmark¶

Hyperparameter ranges follow the professor's recommended guidelines:

  • max_depth: 3–7
  • learning_rate: 0.01–0.1
  • n_estimators: 200–2000 (with early stopping)
  • subsample: 0.7–1.0
  • Huber loss (reg:pseudohubererror) — tested but not suitable for log-price targets (see results below)
In [51]:
# XGBoost: RandomizedSearchCV
xgb_param_grid = {
    'max_depth'        : [3, 4, 5, 6, 7],
    'learning_rate'    : [0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.08, 0.1],
    'n_estimators'     : [300, 500, 800, 1000, 1500],
    'subsample'        : [0.7, 0.75, 0.8, 0.85, 0.9, 1.0],
    'colsample_bytree' : [0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 1.0],
    'min_child_weight' : [1, 3, 5, 7],
    'gamma'            : [0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5],
    'reg_alpha'        : [0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1],
    'reg_lambda'       : [0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0],
}

xgb_base = xgb.XGBRegressor(
    objective='reg:squarederror', random_state=42,
    n_jobs=-1, tree_method='hist'
)

rs_xgb = RandomizedSearchCV(
    xgb_base, xgb_param_grid,
    n_iter=80, cv=KF, scoring=mape_scorer,
    n_jobs=-1, random_state=42, verbose=1, refit=True
)
rs_xgb.fit(X_train[core_features], y_train)

print("\nBest XGBoost params:")
for k, v in rs_xgb.best_params_.items():
    print(f"  {k:20s}: {v}")

Fitting 5 folds for each of 80 candidates, totalling 400 fits

Best XGBoost params:
  subsample           : 0.8
  reg_lambda          : 0.5
  reg_alpha           : 0.1
  n_estimators        : 1500
  min_child_weight    : 7
  max_depth           : 7
  learning_rate       : 0.02
  gamma               : 0.1
  colsample_bytree    : 0.8
In [ ]:
# XGBoost with Huber loss

# Base tuned params
best_p = rs_xgb.best_params_.copy()

# Standard XGBoost
xgb_std = rs_xgb.best_estimator_

results['21_XGB'] = evaluate(
    xgb_std,
    X_train[core_features],
    y_train,
    X_test[core_features],
    y_test,
    '21. XGBoost (tuned)',
    'Non-linear boosted trees (squared error baseline)'
)

# XGBoost Huber Loss
xgb_huber = xgb.XGBRegressor(
    objective='reg:pseudohubererror',
    random_state=42,
    n_jobs=-1,
    tree_method='hist',
    **best_p
)

xgb_huber.fit(X_train[core_features], y_train)

# Scale check: reg:pseudohubererror produces non-finite predictions 
# on log-price targets due to scale mismatch
y_pred_huber_debug = xgb_huber.predict(X_test[core_features])
print(f"Huber pred range : {y_pred_huber_debug.min():.4f} to {y_pred_huber_debug.max():.4f}")
print(f"log_price range  : {y_test.min():.4f} to {y_test.max():.4f}")

y_pred_huber_train = xgb_huber.predict(X_train[core_features])
y_pred_huber_test  = xgb_huber.predict(X_test[core_features])

huber_train_mape = mape(y_train, y_pred_huber_train)
huber_test_mape  = mape(y_test, y_pred_huber_test)
huber_test_r2    = r2_score(y_test, y_pred_huber_test)

cv_huber = cross_val_score(
    xgb_huber,
    X_train[core_features],
    y_train,
    cv=KF,
    scoring=mape_scorer,
    n_jobs=-1
)

cv_mean = float(-np.mean(cv_huber))
cv_std  = float(np.std(cv_huber))

results['22_XGB_Huber'] = dict(
    label='22. XGBoost (Huber loss)',
    train_mape=huber_train_mape,
    test_mape=huber_test_mape,
    test_r2=huber_test_r2,
    cv_mape=cv_mean,
    cv_mape_std=cv_std,
    focus='XGBoost with pseudohubererror objective'
)

print(f"[22. XGBoost Huber] "
      f"Train MAPE: {huber_train_mape:.2f}% | "
      f"Test MAPE: {huber_test_mape:.2f}% | "
      f"R²: {huber_test_r2:.4f} | "
      f"CV MAPE: {cv_mean:.2f}% ± {cv_std:.2f}%")

[21. XGBoost (tuned)]  Train MAPE: 10.30%  Test MAPE: 17.22%  R²: 0.7103  |  CV MAPE: 17.27% ± 1.12%
Huber pred range : 2217.0830 to 2217.0830
log_price range  : 11.5129 to 17.0960
[22. XGBoost Huber] Train MAPE: inf% | Test MAPE: inf% | R²: -16022834.6516 | CV MAPE: inf% ± nan%

XGBoost (squared error vs. Huber loss) serves as the benchmark non-linear model in this experiment and achieves the best overall MAPE, confirming the presence of strong non-linear interactions that linear models cannot capture.

The Huber loss variant (reg:pseudohubererror) was tested but produced non-finite predictions due to scale mismatch with log-price targets. Since our target operates in log-price space, the narrow scale causes invalid gradients and inf predictions, rendering the result non-interpretable. This is consistent with earlier findings: the log-transformation of price already mitigates the effect of outliers, making further robustness unnecessary. As a result, the standard squared error formulation remains the most effective choice for this dataset.

However, compared to linear models, XGBoost shows a larger train–test gap, indicating higher variance and greater sensitivity to overfitting. Even with hyperparameter tuning, tree-based ensembles are inherently harder to regularise to the same stability level as Ridge or ElasticNet in this setting.

In [53]:
# XGBoost learning curve per boosting round
best_p2 = rs_xgb.best_params_.copy()
n_est   = best_p2.pop('n_estimators')
xgb_lc  = xgb.XGBRegressor(objective='reg:squarederror', n_estimators=n_est,
                              random_state=42, n_jobs=-1, tree_method='hist', **best_p2)
xgb_lc.fit(X_train[core_features], y_train,
            eval_set=[(X_train[core_features], y_train),
                      (X_test[core_features], y_test)],
            verbose=False)

step = max(1, n_est // 80)
rounds = list(range(1, n_est + 1, step))
tr_err = [mape(y_train, xgb_lc.predict(X_train[core_features], iteration_range=(0, r))) for r in rounds]
te_err = [mape(y_test,  xgb_lc.predict(X_test[core_features],  iteration_range=(0, r))) for r in rounds]

plt.figure(figsize=(11, 5))
plt.plot(rounds, tr_err, label='Train MAPE', color='royalblue')
plt.plot(rounds, te_err, label='Test MAPE',  color='tomato')
plt.xlabel('Boosting Round'); plt.ylabel('MAPE (%)')
plt.title('XGBoost: Train vs Test MAPE per Boosting Round', fontsize=13)
plt.legend(); plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
print(f"Final Train MAPE: {tr_err[-1]:.2f}%  |  Test MAPE: {te_err[-1]:.2f}%")

Final Train MAPE: 10.31%  |  Test MAPE: 17.22%

The learning curve by boosting rounds shows how train and test MAPE change as more trees are added. Ideally, test MAPE should stabilise without diverging from train MAPE; any increasing gap indicates overfitting.

In [54]:
# XGBoost tuning heatmap
cv_res = pd.DataFrame(rs_xgb.cv_results_)
cv_res['mean_mape'] = -cv_res['mean_test_score']
heat  = cv_res.groupby(['param_max_depth','param_learning_rate'])['mean_mape'].min().unstack()

plt.figure(figsize=(9, 5))
sns.heatmap(heat, annot=True, fmt='.3f', cmap='YlGnBu_r', linewidths=0.4,
            cbar_kws={'label': 'CV MAPE'})
plt.title('XGBoost: CV MAPE by max_depth × learning_rate', fontsize=12)
plt.xlabel('Learning Rate'); plt.ylabel('Max Depth')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
  • Deeper trees (max_depth 6–7) combined with lower learning rates (0.01–0.02) yield the lowest CV MAPE, with the best result at max_depth = 7 and learning_rate = 0.02.
  • Higher learning rates (0.08–0.1) with deep trees tend to produce higher CV MAPE, indicating less stable generalisation.
  • Shallower trees (max_depth 3–4) show mixed results across learning rates, suggesting insufficient model capacity for this dataset.

Deeper trees combined with lower learning rates provide the best balance between bias and variance.

14. Performance Summary & Comparison¶

In [55]:
# summary table of all models
order = [
    '00_Baseline','01_Poly','02_Location','03_Domain','04_Spatial','05_Full_OLS',
    '06_Ridge','07_Lasso','08_ElasticNet','09_Huber','10_BayesianRidge',
    '11_PolyRidge','12_Quantile','13_RFECV_EN','14_PCA_Ridge',
    '15_EqualBlend','16_OptBlend','17_Stacking',
    '18_Spline','19_Spline_EN',
    '20_NCS_EN',
    '21_XGB','22_XGB_Huber',
]
rows = []
for k in order:
    r = results[k]
    rows.append({
        'Model': r['label'],
        'Train MAPE (%)': round(r.get('train_mape', float('nan')), 2),
        'Test MAPE (%)': round(r['test_mape'], 2),
        'CV MAPE (%)': round(r['cv_mape'], 2),
        'CV Std': round(r['cv_mape_std'], 2),
        'Overfit Gap': round(r.get('train_mape', float('nan')) - r['test_mape'], 2),
        'Test R²': round(r['test_r2'], 4),
        'Focus': r.get('focus', '')
    })

summary_df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
summary_df.index = range(1, len(summary_df)+1)

xgb_mape_val = results['21_XGB']['test_mape']
linear_results = {k: results[k] for k in order if 'XGB' not in k}
best_lin_key   = min(linear_results, key=lambda k: linear_results[k]['test_mape'])
best_lin_mape  = linear_results[best_lin_key]['test_mape']

print(f"{'═'*100}")
print(f"{'FULL MODEL PERFORMANCE SUMMARY':^95}")
print(f"{'═'*100}")
display(summary_df)
print(f"{'─'*95}")
print(f"  Best linear model : {results[best_lin_key]['label']}  →  MAPE: {best_lin_mape:.2f}%")
print(f"  XGBoost benchmark : MAPE {xgb_mape_val:.2f}%")
print(f"  Linear–nonlinear gap : {best_lin_mape - xgb_mape_val:.2f}%")
print(f"  Total error reduction (baseline → best linear): {results['00_Baseline']['test_mape'] - best_lin_mape:.2f}%")
print(f"{'═'*95}")

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
                                FULL MODEL PERFORMANCE SUMMARY                                 
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Model Train MAPE (%) Test MAPE (%) CV MAPE (%) CV Std Overfit Gap Test R² Focus
1 00. Baseline OLS 30.4000 30.5900 30.5900 1.8000 -0.1900 0.4796 Raw data only (label encoded)
2 01. OLS + Polynomial/Interaction 30.4800 28.6700 30.7200 1.3200 1.8200 0.5112 Poly terms + interactions
3 02. OLS + Location Signals 18.4400 18.9300 18.7000 1.2500 -0.4900 0.6862 KFold target encoding city/zip
4 03. OLS + Domain Ratios 18.2300 18.9100 18.6000 1.3100 -0.6900 0.6881 Domain metrics + binary indicators
5 04. OLS + KNN Spatial + Extended 17.6500 18.2300 18.1200 0.9900 -0.5800 0.6955 KNN proxy + KMeans + all interactions
6 05. OLS Full Core (45 features) 17.6500 18.2300 18.1200 0.9900 -0.5800 0.6955 All 45 core features
7 06. Ridge (L2 tuned) 17.6800 18.2600 18.0600 1.0300 -0.5800 0.6948 L2 regularisation, tuned alpha
8 07. Lasso (L1 tuned) 17.7100 18.2400 18.0700 1.0400 -0.5300 0.6951 L1 feature selection, tuned alpha
9 08. ElasticNet (tuned) 17.7000 18.2500 18.0700 1.0500 -0.5400 0.6951 L1+L2, grid-searched alpha & l1_ratio
10 09. Huber (tuned) 19.3600 19.4400 19.6900 0.7200 -0.0700 0.5746 Robust to outliers, tuned epsilon
11 10. Bayesian Ridge 17.7200 18.2300 18.0800 1.0700 -0.5100 0.6957 Automatic regularisation via marginal likelihood
12 11. Ridge Regression with Polynomial Interacti... 17.3100 17.9100 17.9000 1.1400 -0.6000 0.6981 Degree-2 poly + RobustScaler + Ridge, alpha=50.0
13 12. Quantile Regression (median) 17.8000 18.3100 18.2600 1.0600 -0.5100 0.6961 Median regression, robust to price outliers
14 13. ElasticNet + RFECV 17.7200 18.2800 18.0400 1.0700 -0.5600 0.6950 RFECV selected 35 features
15 14. PCA + Ridge 43.9500 42.1300 43.9800 3.6900 1.8200 0.0915 PCA 95% variance + Ridge alpha=100.0
16 15. Equal-Weight Blend (5 models) 18.1300 18.2800 18.1300 0.9900 -0.1500 0.6917 Average of ElasticNet, Lasso, Ridge, Huber, Ba...
17 16. Optimal-Weight Blend 18.0600 18.2500 18.0600 0.0000 -0.1900 0.6950 SLSQP-optimised weights on OOF predictions
18 17. Stacking (ElasticNet meta) 18.0300 18.1700 18.0800 1.1100 -0.1400 0.6962 OOF stacking with ElasticNet meta-learner
19 18. Spline + Lasso (tuned) 17.7100 18.4500 18.0900 1.0500 -0.7400 0.6901 Truncated power basis, 6 inner knots
20 19. Spline + ElasticNet 17.7200 18.4300 18.0900 1.0600 -0.7100 0.6909 Truncated power basis + L1+L2 regularization
21 20. NCS + ElasticNet 17.6700 18.2500 18.0900 1.0500 -0.5800 0.6948 Natural Cubic Spline (df=8) + ElasticNet, para...
22 21. XGBoost (tuned) 10.3000 17.2200 17.2700 1.1200 -6.9100 0.7103 Non-linear boosted trees (squared error baseline)
23 22. XGBoost (Huber loss) inf inf inf NaN NaN -16022834.6516 XGBoost with pseudohubererror objective 
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Best linear model : 11. Ridge Regression with Polynomial Interaction Features  →  MAPE: 17.91%
  XGBoost benchmark : MAPE 17.22%
  Linear–nonlinear gap : 0.70%
  Total error reduction (baseline → best linear): 12.68%
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Key Takeaways from the Summary Table¶

  • Regularised linear models outperform raw OLS by ~10 percentage points in Test MAPE once spatial features (target-encoded city/zip and KNN price proxy) are introduced.
