The largest improvement occurs between Stage 1 (structural + polynomial features) and Stage 2 (location signals), confirming that location is a stronger determinant of price than structural attributes alone.
  • The Overfit Gap (Train MAPE − Test MAPE) provides a quick diagnostic of generalisation. A large gap indicates overfitting, while a small gap suggests stable generalisation. Well-regularised models such as Ridge, Lasso, and ElasticNet exhibit near-zero gaps (typically < 0.5%), confirming stable generalisation. PolyRidge, our best linear model, maintains a similarly small gap despite its higher model complexity. XGBoost shows a larger gap (typically 7%), which is expected for high-capacity tree ensembles even after tuning.
  • The performance gap between the best linear model and the XGBoost benchmark quantifies the benefit of non-linear modelling, typically around 0.7% in this experiment. This suggests that while linear models capture most of the signal, there remains additional structure from feature interactions that is better captured by non-linear approaches.
In [56]:
# Modelling journey plot
fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(20, 8))
labels    = [r['label'] for r in [results[k] for k in order]]
mape_vals = [r['test_mape'] for r in [results[k] for k in order]]
r2_vals   = [r['test_r2']   for r in [results[k] for k in order]]
x         = np.arange(len(labels))

ax1.plot(x, mape_vals, 'o-', color='#004C99', lw=2.5, ms=6, label='Test MAPE (%)')
ax1.fill_between(x, mape_vals, alpha=0.07, color='#004C99')
ax1.set_ylabel('MAPE (%) — lower is better', color='#004C99', fontsize=12)
ax1.tick_params(axis='y', labelcolor='#004C99')
ax1.set_xticks(x)
ax1.set_xticklabels(labels, rotation=40, ha='right', fontsize=8)

ax2 = ax1.twinx()
ax2.plot(x, r2_vals, 's--', color='#D35400', lw=2, ms=6, alpha=0.7, label='Test R²')
ax2.set_ylabel('R² — higher is better', color='#D35400', fontsize=12)
ax2.tick_params(axis='y', labelcolor='#D35400')

best_idx = mape_vals.index(best_lin_mape)
ax1.scatter([best_idx], [best_lin_mape], s=300, color='green', zorder=5,
            label=f'Best Linear: {best_lin_mape:.2f}%')
xgb_idx = order.index('21_XGB')
ax1.scatter([xgb_idx], [xgb_mape_val], s=300, color='purple', zorder=5,
            label=f'XGBoost: {xgb_mape_val:.2f}%')

l1, lb1 = ax1.get_legend_handles_labels()
l2, lb2 = ax2.get_legend_handles_labels()
ax1.legend(l1+l2, lb1+lb2, loc='upper right', fontsize=9)
plt.title('Full Modelling Journey: MAPE & R² Across 23 Iterations', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

The Modeling Journey Plot¶

The MAPE curve drops sharply at Stages 2–4 (location encoding and spatial features), then flattens for the regularisation variants. This tells us feature quality drove more improvement than model choice — a common finding in tabular regression. The ensemble models (15–17) show marginal gains over the best single model, suggesting the base models are already highly correlated in their predictions.

In [57]:
# Train vs Test MAPE comparison for top 5 linear models + XGBoost
top5_linear = sorted(linear_results, key=lambda k: linear_results[k]['test_mape'])[:5]
top5_keys   = top5_linear + ['21_XGB']

names      = [results[k]['label'].split('.')[1].strip() for k in top5_keys]
train_mapes = [results[k].get('train_mape', 0) for k in top5_keys]
test_mapes  = [results[k]['test_mape'] for k in top5_keys]

x = np.arange(len(names))
w = 0.35

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 5))
bars1 = ax.bar(x - w/2, train_mapes, width=w, label='Train MAPE', color='#5DADE2', edgecolor='white')
bars2 = ax.bar(x + w/2, test_mapes,  width=w, label='Test MAPE',  color='#1A5276', edgecolor='white')

for bar, v in zip(bars1, train_mapes):
    ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, bar.get_height() + 0.1,
            f'{v:.1f}%', ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=8.5)
for bar, v in zip(bars2, test_mapes):
    ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, bar.get_height() + 0.1,
            f'{v:.1f}%', ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=8.5)

ax.set_ylabel('MAPE (%)')
ax.set_title('Train vs Test MAPE — Top 5 Linear Models + XGBoost', fontsize=13)
ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(names, rotation=20, ha='right')
ax.legend()
ax.set_ylim(0, max(train_mapes + test_mapes) * 1.25)

for bar in [bars1[-1], bars2[-1]]:
    bar.set_color('#8E44AD')
    bar.set_edgecolor('white')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The Train vs. Test MAPE bar chart is a quick overfit check. A small gap (train = test) indicates well-regularised models. XGBoost typically shows a wider gap due to its higher model capacity.

15. Model Interpretability & Diagnostics¶

In [58]:
# Coefficient plot: Best Ridge + Polynomial model

poly_model = results['11_PolyRidge']['model']
inner_en = poly_model.model

poly_feature_names = poly_model.poly.get_feature_names_out(poly_model.poly_cols)
all_features = list(poly_feature_names) + poly_model.remaining_cols

coef_df = pd.DataFrame({'Feature': all_features, 'Coefficient': inner_en.coef_})
coef_df['AbsCoef'] = coef_df['Coefficient'].abs()
coef_df = coef_df.sort_values('AbsCoef', ascending=False)

n_zero = (coef_df['AbsCoef'] < 1e-8).sum()
print(f"Poly+Ridge zeroed out {n_zero} features (out of {len(all_features)})")
print(f"\nTop 15 features:")

top15 = coef_df.head(15)
colors_bar = ['#6DB6FF' if v > 0 else '#FF7F7F' for v in top15['Coefficient']]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 8))
bars = ax.barh(top15['Feature'][::-1], top15['Coefficient'][::-1],
               color=colors_bar[::-1], edgecolor='white')

ax.axvline(0, color='black', lw=1)
ax.set_title('Poly+Ridge: Top 15 Standardised Coefficients', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_xlabel('Coefficient (Scaled + Polynomial Features)')

for bar, v in zip(bars, top15['Coefficient'][::-1]):
    ha = 'left' if v >= 0 else 'right'
    offset = 0.002 if v >= 0 else -0.002
    ax.text(v + offset,
            bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()/2,
            f'{v:.4f}',
            va='center', ha=ha, fontsize=9)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Poly+Ridge zeroed out 0 features (out of 73)

Top 15 features:

The Ridge model with polynomial features reveals the following key drivers for property value:

  • Primary Driver: zipcode_price_signal is the strongest positive predictor of property value.
  • Neighborhood & City Signals: neighbor_price_proxy and city_price_signal also contribute significantly, reflecting the strong influence of local market conditions and city-level price premiums.
  • Structural Size: Features such as sqft_above_log, sqft_living_log, and sqft_living_sqrt show meaningful positive contributions, confirming that living area remains a key structural driver.
  • Interaction Effects: Terms such as living_x_cond, zipcode_price_signal × house_age, and condition × house_age improve predictive performance by capturing combined effects between structural, temporal, and location features.
  • Negative Drivers: cluster_label and city_price_signal^2 show negative effects, reflecting lower-priced geographic zones and diminishing returns on city-level location premium respectively.

The Ridge model highlights location-based signals (zipcode, neighborhood, and city) as the dominant drivers of price, while interaction terms provide additional refinement to capture more nuanced relationships.

In [59]:
# Permutation Importance: PolyRidge

poly_model = results['11_PolyRidge']['model']

# baseline score
base_score = mape(y_test, poly_model.predict(X_test))

importances = []
stds = []

for col in X_test.columns:
    scores = []

    for _ in range(20):  # n_repeats
        X_perm = X_test.copy()
        X_perm[col] = np.random.permutation(X_perm[col].values)

        score = mape(y_test, poly_model.predict(X_perm))
        scores.append(score)

    importances.append(np.mean(scores) - base_score)
    stds.append(np.std(scores))

perm_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Feature': X_test.columns,
    'Importance': importances,
    'Std': stds
}).sort_values('Importance', ascending=False).head(20)

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 7))
plt.barh(
    perm_df['Feature'][::-1],
    perm_df['Importance'][::-1],
    xerr=perm_df['Std'][::-1],
    color='#95C8D8',
    edgecolor='white',
    capsize=3
)

plt.title('Permutation Importance: PolyRidge', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Increase in MAPE when feature is shuffled')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Permutation Importance — PolyRidge

  • Top Driver: zipcode_price_signal is by far the most important predictor — permuting it causes an increase of ~7.5% in MAPE, far exceeding all other features.
  • View & Interaction Effects: living_x_view and log_living_x_view rank second and fourth, indicating that the combined effect of living area and view quality is a strong price signal.
  • City & Neighborhood Signals: city_price_signal and neighbor_price_proxy also show strong predictive importance, confirming that location at multiple levels (zip, city, neighborhood) drives price.
  • Living Space Features: Variables such as effective_living, sqft_living_sqrt, sqft_above, sqft_living, and sqft_living_log contribute meaningfully, but are secondary to spatial and view signals.
  • Lower Impact: Features like age_category, bathrooms, sqft_per_room, avg_room_size, house_age, years_since_renovation, and bed_x_sqft have relatively minor influence on predictions.

Permutation importance confirms that location-based signals (zipcode, city, and neighborhood) are the dominant drivers of price, while view-related and structural features provide additional but secondary explanatory power.

In [60]:
# Actual vs Predicted + Residual PolyRidge

y_pred_best = results['11_PolyRidge'].get('y_pred', None)

if y_pred_best is None:
    y_pred_best = poly_model.predict(X_test)

y_actual_price = np.expm1(y_test)
y_pred_price   = np.expm1(y_pred_best)

y_actual_price = np.nan_to_num(y_actual_price)
y_pred_price   = np.nan_to_num(y_pred_price)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(20, 5))

# Actual vs Predicted
axes[0].scatter(y_actual_price, y_pred_price, alpha=0.3, s=10, color='#27AE60')

lim = [
    min(np.min(y_actual_price), np.min(y_pred_price)),
    max(np.max(y_actual_price), np.max(y_pred_price))
]

axes[0].plot(lim, lim, 'r--', lw=2)
axes[0].set_title('PolyRidge: Actual vs Predicted', fontsize=11)
axes[0].set_xlabel('Actual Price ($)')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Predicted Price ($)')

axes[0].xaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f'${x/1e6:.1f}M'))
axes[0].yaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f'${x/1e6:.1f}M'))

# Residual distribution
eps = 1e-9
res_pct = ((y_actual_price - y_pred_price) / (y_actual_price + eps)) * 100

axes[1].hist(res_pct, bins=70, color='#E67E22', edgecolor='white')
axes[1].axvline(0, color='red', linestyle='--', lw=1.5)
axes[1].set_title('Percentage Residual Distribution', fontsize=11)
axes[1].set_xlabel('Error (%)')
axes[1].set_xlim(-100, 100)

# Residual vs Predicted
raw_res = y_actual_price - y_pred_price

axes[2].scatter(y_pred_price, raw_res, alpha=0.3, s=10, color='#8E44AD')
axes[2].axhline(0, color='red', linestyle='--', lw=1.5)
axes[2].set_title('PolyRidge: Residuals vs Predicted', fontsize=11)
axes[2].set_xlabel('Predicted Price ($)')
axes[2].set_ylabel('Residual Error ($)')

axes[2].xaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f'${x/1e6:.1f}M'))

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(f"Median |% error|: {np.median(np.abs(res_pct)):.2f}%")

Median |% error|: 11.54%

Actual vs. Predicted | Residual Distribution | Residuals vs. Predicted

  • Actual vs Predicted: Predictions should align with the 45 degree diagonal. The widening spread at higher price levels indicates systematic under-prediction of luxury homes, a common limitation of linear models under skewed targets.
  • Residual Distribution: Errors are largely centred around zero, but a slight right skew suggests occasional large under-predictions for ultra-high-end properties.
  • Residuals vs Predicted: The residuals are relatively evenly distributed around zero, indicating stable variance after log transformation, with no strong funnel pattern remaining.

PolyRidge achieves a median absolute percentage error of 11.5%, but still underestimates extreme luxury properties due to residual target skewness at the upper tail.

In [61]:
# Sklearn Wrapper
class SklearnWrapper(BaseEstimator, RegressorMixin):
    def __init__(self, model):
        self.model = model

    def fit(self, X, y):
        self.model.fit(X, y)
        return self

    def predict(self, X):
        return self.model.predict(X)


# Wrap PolyRidge model
poly_model = SklearnWrapper(results['11_PolyRidge']['model'])


# Learning Curve
tr_sz, tr_sc, va_sc = learning_curve(
    poly_model,
    X_train,
    y_train,
    cv=5,
    scoring=mape_scorer,
    n_jobs=-1,
    train_sizes=np.linspace(0.1, 1.0, 12)
)

tr_m = (-tr_sc).mean(axis=1)
va_m = (-va_sc).mean(axis=1)


# Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))

plt.plot(tr_sz, tr_m, 'o-', color='red', label='Train MAPE')
plt.plot(tr_sz, va_m, 'o-', color='green', label='CV MAPE')

plt.fill_between(
    tr_sz,
    tr_m - (-tr_sc).std(axis=1),
    tr_m + (-tr_sc).std(axis=1),
    alpha=0.1,
    color='red'
)

plt.fill_between(
    tr_sz,
    va_m - (-va_sc).std(axis=1),
    va_m + (-va_sc).std(axis=1),
    alpha=0.1,
    color='green'
)

plt.xlabel('Training Set Size')
plt.ylabel('MAPE (%)')
plt.title('PolyRidge Learning Curve', fontsize=13)
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Learning Curve — PolyRidge

  • Train MAPE Initially low due to memorisation on small samples, then increases as more diverse data is introduced.
  • CV MAPE Starts higher due to underfitting on limited data, then decreases and converges toward the training curve as sample size grows.
  • Convergence: Both curves stabilise around ~17–18% MAPE, indicating the model’s performance ceiling given the current feature set and complexity.

PolyRidge generalises well under Ridge regularisation, as shown by the small train–CV gap. However, performance gains diminish beyond ~2000 samples, suggesting limited additional benefit from increasing data size without further feature improvements.

In [62]:
# Market alignment density: PolyRidge vs XGBoost vs Actual

poly_pred = results['11_PolyRidge'].get('y_pred', None)
xgb_pred  = results['21_XGB'].get('y_pred', None)

if poly_pred is None:
    poly_pred = poly_model.predict(X_test)

if xgb_pred is None:
    xgb_pred = xgb_std.predict(X_test[core_features])

y_actual_price = np.expm1(y_test)
poly_pred_price = np.expm1(poly_pred)
xgb_pred_price  = np.expm1(xgb_pred)

y_actual_price = np.nan_to_num(y_actual_price)
poly_pred_price = np.nan_to_num(poly_pred_price)
xgb_pred_price  = np.nan_to_num(xgb_pred_price)

# clip extreme values for stability
clip_max = 2_500_000
y_actual_price = np.clip(y_actual_price, 0, clip_max)
poly_pred_price = np.clip(poly_pred_price, 0, clip_max)
xgb_pred_price  = np.clip(xgb_pred_price, 0, clip_max)


fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 5))

for ax, xlim, title in zip(
    axes,
    [(0, 2_500_000), (200_000, 1_200_000)],
    ['Full Price Range', 'Core Market ($200k–$1.2M)']
):

    sns.kdeplot(y_actual_price, ax=ax,
                label='Actual', fill=True,
                color='#27AE60', alpha=0.5)

    sns.kdeplot(poly_pred_price, ax=ax,
                label='PolyRidge', fill=True,
                color='#F39C12', alpha=0.4)

    sns.kdeplot(xgb_pred_price, ax=ax,
                label='XGBoost', fill=True,
                color='#9B59B6', alpha=0.35)

    ax.set_xlim(xlim)
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=12)
    ax.set_xlabel('House Price ($)')

    ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(
        mtick.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f'${x/1e3:.0f}K')
    )

    ax.legend()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Price Distribution Alignment — PolyRidge vs. XGBoost vs. Actual

This KDE plot checks how well each model's predicted price distribution matches the actual market distribution.

  • Full Range: PolyRidge tends to compress extreme values, leading to under-representation of both luxury and low-end properties. In contrast, XGBoost captures tail behavior more accurately.
  • Core Market (200K–1.2M): Both models closely align with the actual distribution, indicating good calibration within the typical price range.

PolyRidge prioritises stability and smoothness, which reduces variance but also narrows the predicted spread. XGBoost better preserves market variability, particularly at higher price levels.

In [63]:
# Coefficients and VIF for PolyRidge

poly_model_obj = results['11_PolyRidge']['model']

poly_part = poly_model_obj.poly
sc_poly   = poly_model_obj.sc_poly
sc_rest   = poly_model_obj.sc_rest
ridge     = poly_model_obj.model

poly_cols_local = poly_model_obj.poly_cols
remaining_core_local = poly_model_obj.remaining_cols

Xp = sc_poly.transform(X_train[poly_cols_local].values)
Xp = poly_part.transform(Xp)

feature_names_poly = poly_part.get_feature_names_out(poly_cols_local)

Xr = sc_rest.transform(X_train[remaining_core_local].values)

X_full = np.hstack([Xp, Xr])

feature_names_all = np.concatenate([feature_names_poly, remaining_core_local])

coef_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "feature": feature_names_all,
    "coef": ridge.coef_
})

coef_df["abs_coef"] = coef_df["coef"].abs()

top10 = coef_df.sort_values("abs_coef", ascending=False).head(10)

print("\n Top 10 Important Features (PolyRidge):")
print(top10[["feature", "coef"]].to_string(index=False))

top10_sorted = top10.sort_values("coef")

plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
plt.barh(top10_sorted["feature"], top10_sorted["coef"])
plt.title("Top 10 Important Features (PolyRidge Coefficients)")
plt.xlabel("Coefficient Value")
plt.ylabel("Feature")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# VIF Calculation (Polynomial only, sample n=500 for speed)
np.random.seed(42)

sample_idx = np.random.choice(Xp.shape[0], size=min(500, Xp.shape[0]), replace=False)
Xp_sample = Xp[sample_idx, :]

n_vif = min(15, Xp_sample.shape[1])

vif_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "feature": feature_names_poly[:n_vif],
    "VIF": [
        variance_inflation_factor(Xp_sample[:, :n_vif], i)
        for i in range(n_vif)
    ]
})

vif_df = vif_df.sort_values("VIF", ascending=False)

print("\n VIF (Top Polynomial Features, sample n=500):")
print(vif_df.head(10).to_string(index=False))

vif_plot = vif_df.sort_values("VIF", ascending=True)

plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
plt.barh(vif_plot["feature"], vif_plot["VIF"])
plt.title("VIF of Polynomial Features (Multicollinearity Check)")
plt.xlabel("VIF")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
plt.hist(coef_df["coef"], bins=30)
plt.title("Distribution of Ridge Coefficients")
plt.xlabel("Coefficient Value")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

 Top 10 Important Features (PolyRidge):
             feature    coef
zipcode_price_signal  0.2274
       cluster_label -0.1015
neighbor_price_proxy  0.0955
      sqft_above_log  0.0638
     sqft_living_log  0.0633
       living_x_cond  0.0628
   city_price_signal  0.0599
    sqft_living_sqrt  0.0592
        age_category  0.0564
         is_historic  0.0544

 VIF (Top Polynomial Features, sample n=500):
                             feature     VIF
                zipcode_price_signal 18.1421
sqft_living_log zipcode_price_signal 16.3357
sqft_living_log neighbor_price_proxy 15.4279
                neighbor_price_proxy 14.8305
                   city_price_signal  4.1853
   sqft_living_log city_price_signal  4.0571
                sqft_living_log view  3.0391
                                view  2.7202
                   sqft_living_log^2  2.6075
                     sqft_living_log  2.3575

Top 10 Coefficients & VIF — PolyRidge

  • Coefficient bar chart: Shows the direction and magnitude of each top feature's impact on log_price. zipcode_price_signal dominates with the largest positive coefficient, followed by neighbor_price_proxy and structural size features. cluster_label is the only notable negative coefficient, reflecting lower-priced geographic zones.

  • VIF (Variance Inflation Factor): Measures multicollinearity between features. A VIF > 10 indicates that a feature's variance is inflated due to strong correlation with other variables. Spatial features such as zipcode_price_signal and its polynomial interaction terms show the highest VIF, which is expected given their strong correlation with each other. Ridge regularisation explicitly handles multicollinearity by shrinking correlated coefficients, so VIF is used here for transparency rather than as a feature selection criterion.

  • Coefficient distribution: The histogram shows that most coefficients are concentrated near zero, confirming Ridge's shrinkage effect. The single large positive outlier corresponds to zipcode_price_signal, reinforcing its dominant role in the model.

Model Interpretability: What Drives House Prices Here?¶

Across both coefficient analysis and permutation importance, location emerges as the primary driver — zipcode_price_signal dominates in both metrics, confirming that zip code-level pricing signal is the single most influential factor. Spatial features at multiple levels (zipcode, city, neighborhood) collectively account for the largest share of predictive power.

Permutation importance additionally reveals that view-related interactions (living_x_view, log_living_x_view) rank higher than raw structural size features — a nuance not visible in coefficients alone, where size features appear more prominent.

The learning curve shows train and CV MAPE converging around 2,000 samples — adding more data beyond that point yields diminishing returns, which is consistent with a model that's approaching its representational ceiling on these features.

16. Final Conclusion & Verdict¶

In [64]:
# Conclusion and Summary Table
order = [
    '00_Baseline','01_Poly','02_Location','03_Domain','04_Spatial','05_Full_OLS',
    '06_Ridge','07_Lasso','08_ElasticNet','09_Huber','10_BayesianRidge',
    '11_PolyRidge','12_Quantile','13_RFECV_EN','14_PCA_Ridge',
    '15_EqualBlend','16_OptBlend','17_Stacking',
    '18_Spline','19_Spline_EN',
    '20_NCS_EN',
    '21_XGB','22_XGB_Huber',
]

rows = []
for k in order:
    r = results[k]
    rows.append({
        'Key': k,
        'Model': r['label'],
        'Train MAPE (%)': round(r.get('train_mape', float('nan')), 2),
        'Test MAPE (%)': round(r['test_mape'], 2),
        'CV MAPE (%)': round(r['cv_mape'], 2),
        'Test R²': round(r['test_r2'], 4),
    })

summary_df = pd.DataFrame(rows)

def highlight_models(row):
    if row['Key'] in ['11_PolyRidge', '21_XGB']:
        return [
            'outline: 3px solid red; font-weight: bold;'
        ] * len(row)
    return [''] * len(row)

from IPython.display import display

styled_df = summary_df.style.apply(highlight_models, axis=1)

print(f"{'═'*80}")
print(f"{'MODEL PERFORMANCE SUMMARY':^80}")
print(f"{'═'*80}")

display(
    styled_df
    .hide(axis='index')
    .format({
        'Train MAPE (%)': '{:.2f}',
        'Test MAPE (%)': '{:.2f}',
        'CV MAPE (%)': '{:.2f}',
        'Test R²': '{:.4f}'
    })
)

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
                           MODEL PERFORMANCE SUMMARY                            
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Key Model Train MAPE (%) Test MAPE (%) CV MAPE (%) Test R²
00_Baseline 00. Baseline OLS 30.40 30.59 30.59 0.4796
01_Poly 01. OLS + Polynomial/Interaction 30.48 28.67 30.72 0.5112
02_Location 02. OLS + Location Signals 18.44 18.93 18.70 0.6862
03_Domain 03. OLS + Domain Ratios 18.23 18.91 18.60 0.6881
04_Spatial 04. OLS + KNN Spatial + Extended 17.65 18.23 18.12 0.6955
05_Full_OLS 05. OLS Full Core (45 features) 17.65 18.23 18.12 0.6955
06_Ridge 06. Ridge (L2 tuned) 17.68 18.26 18.06 0.6948
07_Lasso 07. Lasso (L1 tuned) 17.71 18.24 18.07 0.6951
08_ElasticNet 08. ElasticNet (tuned) 17.70 18.25 18.07 0.6951
09_Huber 09. Huber (tuned) 19.36 19.44 19.69 0.5746
10_BayesianRidge 10. Bayesian Ridge 17.72 18.23 18.08 0.6957
11_PolyRidge 11. Ridge Regression with Polynomial Interaction Features 17.31 17.91 17.90 0.6981
12_Quantile 12. Quantile Regression (median) 17.80 18.31 18.26 0.6961
13_RFECV_EN 13. ElasticNet + RFECV 17.72 18.28 18.04 0.6950
14_PCA_Ridge 14. PCA + Ridge 43.95 42.13 43.98 0.0915
15_EqualBlend 15. Equal-Weight Blend (5 models) 18.13 18.28 18.13 0.6917
16_OptBlend 16. Optimal-Weight Blend 18.06 18.25 18.06 0.6950
17_Stacking 17. Stacking (ElasticNet meta) 18.03 18.17 18.08 0.6962
18_Spline 18. Spline + Lasso (tuned) 17.71 18.45 18.09 0.6901
19_Spline_EN 19. Spline + ElasticNet 17.72 18.43 18.09 0.6909
20_NCS_EN 20. NCS + ElasticNet 17.67 18.25 18.09 0.6948
21_XGB 21. XGBoost (tuned) 10.30 17.22 17.27 0.7103
22_XGB_Huber 22. XGBoost (Huber loss) inf inf inf -16022834.6516

Result > Best Model: Ridge + Polynomial Features (PolyRidge)¶

After evaluating 23 models across five modelling strategies — baseline OLS, regularised linear models, advanced linear variants, ensemble methods, and a non-linear XGBoost benchmark — the Ridge + Polynomial (degree-2) model (Model 11) emerged as the best linear model, delivering the lowest Test MAPE among all linear approaches.

Verdict¶

PolyRidge is the recommended production model among linear approaches, as it offers a strong balance of interpretability (signed coefficients and permutation importance), stable generalisation (low overfit gap), and competitive performance within ~0.7% of the non-linear benchmark (XGBoost Benchmark).

If prediction accuracy is the sole priority, XGBoost is preferred. However, when interpretability, deployment simplicity, or regulatory constraints are important, PolyRidge is the more suitable choice.

17. Future Work¶

Here are several directions that could further improve performance and robustness:

1. Improved Spatial Features¶

  • Incorporate latitude/longitude instead of only city/zip-level signals
  • Add distance-based features (e.g. proximity to schools, MRT, parks)
  • Explore spatial modeling approaches to better capture neighbourhood structure

2. External Data Sources¶

  • Include macroeconomic indicators (interest rates, CPI, housing supply)
  • Add neighbourhood-level context such as school quality and crime rates
  • Use external pricing references (e.g. listing estimates) as additional signals

3. Stronger Modeling Approaches¶

  • Experiment with LightGBM / CatBoost for improved categorical and non-linear handling
  • Test simple neural networks to capture higher-order interactions
  • Explore probabilistic models (e.g. Gaussian Processes) for uncertainty estimation

4. Temporal Information¶

  • Incorporate transaction dates to capture market trends over time
  • Use time-aware validation (e.g. rolling or forward chaining splits)

5. Deployment Enhancements¶

  • Provide prediction intervals instead of point estimates
  • Add SHAP explanations for more interpretable predictions
